title: Chicken Biryani
description: A practical home-style Chicken Biryani recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp saffron soaked in 2 tbsp warm milk
steps: Rinse the basmati rice until the water is nearly clear, soak it for 30 minutes and drain. Bring a large pot of salted water with the whole spices to a rolling boil.
Heat the oil or ghee in a heavy biryani pot over medium heat. Fry the sliced onions for 10 to 12 minutes until deep golden; remove half and drain for layering.
Add ginger-garlic paste to the pot and cook for 1 minute. Add the chicken and sear over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes until the surfaces lose their raw colour.
Add tomatoes, yogurt, biryani masala, chilli and turmeric. Cook uncovered for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring often, until the tomatoes collapse and the oil separates from a thick masala.
Add a small splash of water, cover and simmer for 12 to 15 minutes until the chicken is almost cooked. Stir in half the mint, coriander and lemon juice; the masala should be thick, not watery.
Boil the soaked rice for 5 to 7 minutes until the grains are about 70% cooked—soft outside with a firm centre—then drain immediately.
Spread the rice over the chicken. Top with reserved onions, remaining herbs and saffron milk. Cover tightly, place over medium heat for 5 minutes, then steam on the lowest heat for 18 to 20 minutes.
Turn off the heat and rest covered for 10 minutes. Open from the side, lift rice and chicken gently without crushing the grains and serve hot.

title: Beef Biryani
description: A practical home-style Beef Biryani recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Wash and soak the basmati rice for 30 minutes. Fry the sliced onions in oil or ghee until dark golden, then reserve half for the final layers.
Add ginger-garlic paste to the remaining onions and cook for 1 minute. Add beef cubes and brown over medium-high heat for 8 to 10 minutes.
Stir in tomatoes, yogurt, biryani masala and chilli. Cook for 12 minutes, stirring until the masala thickens and oil appears at the edges.
Add 2 to 3 cups hot water, cover and simmer for 60 to 90 minutes, or pressure-cook for 25 to 30 minutes, until the beef is tender. Reduce the gravy until it coats the meat.
Bring plenty of salted water to a boil with whole spices. Add the drained rice and boil until about 70% cooked, then drain without rinsing.
Layer half the rice over the beef, scatter mint, coriander, lemon and some fried onions, then add the remaining rice and garnishes.
Cover tightly and steam for 5 minutes over medium heat followed by 22 to 25 minutes over the lowest heat.
Rest covered for 12 minutes, then fluff gently from the sides so the tender beef remains intact.

title: Sindhi Biryani
description: A practical home-style Sindhi Biryani recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse and soak the rice for 30 minutes. Fry the onions in oil until deep brown and crisp; reserve one-third for layering.
Add ginger-garlic paste and the chicken or mutton and sear for 7 to 10 minutes. Add tomatoes, yogurt, chilli, turmeric, cumin and biryani spices.
Cook the masala over medium heat for 12 to 15 minutes until the tomatoes break down and oil separates.
Add potatoes, dried plums and enough hot water for the meat to cook. Cover and simmer until the meat and potatoes are almost tender—about 20 minutes for chicken or 50 to 70 minutes for mutton.
Add green chillies, mint, coriander and lemon. Reduce until the masala is intensely flavoured and only slightly moist.
Boil the soaked rice in salted spiced water until 70% cooked and drain promptly.
Layer rice and meat masala, finishing with rice, reserved onions, herbs and saffron or colour. Seal the lid and steam for 20 to 25 minutes on very low heat.
Rest 10 minutes before mixing gently. A proper Sindhi biryani should be spicy, tangy and fragrant with distinct rice grains.

title: Memoni Biryani
description: A practical home-style Memoni Biryani recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Wash the rice and soak for 30 minutes. Fry onions in oil until deep golden and remove half for the top.
Add ginger-garlic paste and the meat, then sear over medium-high heat until lightly browned.
Add chopped tomatoes, yogurt, chilli powder, turmeric, cumin and whole spices. Cook until the masala is thick and glossy.
Add potatoes and enough hot water to tenderise the meat. Simmer covered until the meat is nearly done, then add green chillies, mint, coriander and dried plums if included.
Reduce the gravy firmly; Memoni biryani should not have a loose, tomato-heavy masala. Taste for a distinctly hot, savoury balance.
Parboil the soaked rice in well-salted water until 70% cooked and drain.
Layer the rice over the meat with fried onions, lemon and herbs. Cover tightly and steam for 22 minutes over the lowest heat.
Rest 10 minutes, then lift portions from the bottom so meat, potato and rice remain layered rather than mashed together.

title: Yakhni Pulao
description: A practical home-style Yakhni Pulao recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g beef or mutton with bones
steps: Place the beef or lamb, water, onion, ginger, garlic and whole spices in a pot. Bring to a boil, skim thoroughly and simmer gently until the meat is tender.
Remove the meat and strain the yakhni through a fine sieve. Measure the broth; for 2 cups soaked basmati rice, keep about 3 to 3 1/4 cups broth depending on the rice.
Rinse and soak the rice for 30 minutes, then drain completely.
Heat ghee or oil in a heavy pot and brown the sliced onion. Add cumin and the cooked meat and fry for 3 to 4 minutes.
Add yogurt, ginger-garlic paste and salt and cook until the yogurt loses its raw smell and the fat separates.
Pour in the measured hot yakhni and bring to a strong boil. Add the drained rice and cook uncovered until the liquid falls just below the rice surface.
Cover tightly, reduce to the lowest heat and steam for 15 to 18 minutes.
Rest for 10 minutes, fluff with a fork and serve with the meat distributed through the fragrant, pale rice.

title: Kabuli Pulao
description: A practical home-style Kabuli Pulao recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Simmer the beef or lamb with water, onion, garlic and salt until tender. Strain and reserve enough broth for the rice; keep the meat warm.
Rinse and soak the basmati rice for 30 minutes, then drain.
Cook the carrot strips in a little oil over medium heat for 3 to 4 minutes. Add raisins and sugar and cook for 1 minute until the raisins plump; remove and set aside.
Brown sliced onion in the remaining oil. Add the cooked meat, cumin, cardamom and black pepper and fry for 3 minutes.
Add the measured hot broth and bring to a boil. Add rice and cook uncovered until most liquid is absorbed and small steam holes appear.
Mound the carrot-raisin mixture over the rice without stirring. Cover tightly and steam over the lowest heat for 18 to 20 minutes.
Rest 10 minutes, then transfer rice and meat to a platter without breaking the grains.
Arrange carrots, raisins and nuts over the top so the sweet garnish remains distinct from the savoury rice.

title: Chicken Pulao
description: A practical home-style Chicken Pulao recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the rice thoroughly and soak for 25 to 30 minutes. Drain before cooking.
Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pot and fry the onion until medium brown. Add cumin, cardamom, cinnamon and cloves and stir for 30 seconds.
Add the chicken and ginger-garlic paste and cook over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes until lightly browned.
Add yogurt, salt and black pepper and cook for 4 to 5 minutes until the yogurt is absorbed.
Pour in stock or water and simmer covered for 10 minutes so the chicken is nearly cooked. Measure the liquid and adjust to the amount needed for the rice.
Bring the broth to a boil, add the drained rice and cook uncovered until the liquid drops below the rice surface.
Cover tightly and steam on the lowest heat for 15 minutes. The chicken must reach 74°C (165°F).
Rest for 10 minutes before fluffing gently and serving.

title: Matar Pulao
description: A practical home-style Matar Pulao recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the rice until the water is mostly clear, soak for 20 to 30 minutes and drain.
Heat ghee or oil in a heavy pot. Add cumin, cardamom and cinnamon and let them sizzle for 20 seconds.
Add sliced onion and cook for 7 to 9 minutes until golden.
Stir in the peas and cook for 2 to 3 minutes; fresh peas may need a few minutes longer than frozen peas.
Add the drained rice and fold gently for 1 minute so the grains are coated with fat without breaking.
Pour in the measured hot water or stock and salt. Bring to a strong boil and cook uncovered until the liquid is nearly absorbed.
Cover tightly and steam on the lowest heat for 12 to 15 minutes.
Rest covered for 10 minutes, fluff with a fork and serve while the peas remain bright and the grains separate.

title: Zarda Rice
description: A practical home-style Zarda Rice recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/4 tsp yellow food colour or saffron
steps: Wash the basmati rice and soak for 30 minutes. Bring plenty of water to a boil with yellow colour or saffron and cardamom.
Add the rice and boil until about 90% cooked; the grains should be tender but not splitting. Drain thoroughly.
In a wide heavy pot, melt ghee and fry the cardamom for 30 seconds.
Add sugar and 1/2 cup water and heat gently until the sugar dissolves into a clear syrup; do not caramelise it.
Add the drained rice and fold carefully until evenly coated. Scatter raisins, nuts and candied fruit over the rice.
Cover and cook over the lowest heat for 10 to 12 minutes until the syrup is absorbed, stirring only once from the edge if necessary.
Turn off the heat and rest for 10 minutes so the grains firm and separate.
Fluff gently and serve warm, distributing the nuts and fruit evenly.

title: Tehri
description: A practical home-style Tehri recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the rice and soak for 20 minutes. Peel and cube the potatoes evenly.
Heat oil or ghee and fry the sliced onion until golden. Add cumin, cardamom and cinnamon and stir for 30 seconds.
Add tomato, turmeric, chilli and salt and cook for 4 to 5 minutes until the tomato softens.
Add potatoes and sauté for 4 minutes so their surfaces absorb the spices.
Pour in the measured water or stock, cover and simmer for 8 to 10 minutes until the potatoes are half tender.
Add the drained rice and bring to a boil. Cook uncovered until the liquid drops below the rice.
Cover tightly and steam on very low heat for 15 minutes, then rest for 10 minutes.
Fluff gently so the yellow rice remains separate and the potato cubes stay intact.

title: Chicken Karahi
description: A practical home-style Chicken Karahi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oil in a wide karahi over medium-high heat. Add the chicken and fry for 7 to 9 minutes, turning until lightly browned and no longer pink on the surface.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute without letting it burn.
Add chopped tomatoes, salt, chilli, cumin and coriander. Cover for 8 to 10 minutes until the tomatoes soften and release juice.
Uncover and mash the tomatoes into the chicken. Cook over medium-high heat for 10 to 15 minutes, stirring frequently, until the moisture evaporates and oil separates.
Add a small splash of water only if the chicken is not tender; cover briefly and cook until it reaches 74°C (165°F).
Add crushed black pepper, green chillies and garam masala and cook uncovered for 2 minutes.
Finish with julienned ginger and fresh coriander.
Rest for 5 minutes and serve directly from the karahi with naan.

title: Mutton Karahi
description: A practical home-style Mutton Karahi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy karahi and brown the mutton in batches for 8 to 10 minutes.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute, then add tomatoes, salt, chilli, cumin and coriander.
Cover and cook over medium heat for 12 minutes until the tomatoes collapse.
Add 1 1/2 to 2 cups hot water, cover and simmer gently for 50 to 75 minutes, or pressure-cook for about 20 minutes, until the mutton is tender.
Remove the lid and cook over high heat, stirring and scraping, until the liquid reduces and the tomato masala clings to the meat.
Add crushed black pepper, green chillies and garam masala and fry for another 2 to 3 minutes.
Garnish with ginger and coriander and check the meat is fork-tender.
Rest for 8 minutes before serving with naan.

title: White Karahi
description: A practical home-style White Karahi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g boneless chicken, cut into pieces
steps: Whisk yogurt, cream, cumin, coriander, white pepper, half the black pepper and salt until completely smooth.
Heat oil or ghee in a wide karahi over medium-high heat. Add the boneless chicken and sear for 6 to 8 minutes until lightly golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 45 seconds.
Lower the heat and add the yogurt mixture in three additions, stirring continuously after each so it does not split.
Cover and cook over medium-low heat for 10 to 12 minutes, stirring every few minutes, until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F).
Uncover and cook over medium-high heat for 5 to 7 minutes until the sauce thickens and the fat begins to separate.
Add green chillies, remaining black pepper and garam masala and cook for 2 minutes.
Finish with julienned ginger and coriander and serve immediately.

title: Peshawari Karahi
description: A practical home-style Peshawari Karahi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: Fresh ginger and coriander for garnish
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a wide karahi over high heat and sear the chicken or mutton until well browned on the surface.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add the chopped tomatoes, cumin and salt. Cover and cook until the tomatoes collapse—about 10 minutes for chicken or 15 minutes for mutton.
Mash the tomatoes into the meat and add only enough water to prevent scorching. For mutton, cover and simmer until tender.
Uncover and cook over high heat, stirring frequently, until the tomato moisture evaporates and the oil separates clearly.
Add crushed black pepper and slit green chillies and cook for 3 minutes; Peshawari karahi relies on tomato, pepper and meat rather than a long spice list.
Check chicken reaches 74°C (165°F) or mutton is fork-tender.
Garnish with fresh ginger and coriander and rest 5 minutes before serving.

title: Chicken Korma
description: A practical home-style Chicken Korma recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pot and fry the sliced onions until deep golden. Remove them and grind or crush with a little water.
In the same pot, add whole spices and ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add chicken and fry over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes until lightly browned.
Lower the heat and add whisked yogurt gradually with coriander, chilli and turmeric, stirring constantly to prevent curdling.
Return the crushed fried onions and cook the masala for 8 to 10 minutes until thick and glossy.
Add water, cover and simmer for 15 to 20 minutes until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F) and the gravy is rich.
Add garam masala, kewra or rose water if included and simmer for 2 minutes.
Rest covered for 5 minutes and serve with naan or pulao.

title: Mutton Korma
description: A practical home-style Mutton Korma recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Fry the sliced onions in ghee or oil until deep golden, then remove and grind them with a little water or yogurt.
Add whole spices and ginger-garlic paste to the same pot and stir for 1 minute.
Add mutton and brown over medium-high heat for 8 to 10 minutes.
Lower the heat and add whisked yogurt, coriander, chilli and turmeric gradually, stirring until the yogurt is absorbed.
Return the fried-onion paste and cook for 10 minutes until the masala darkens and fat separates.
Add hot water, cover and simmer for 60 to 90 minutes, or pressure-cook for 25 minutes, until the mutton is fork-tender.
Reduce the gravy to a silky consistency, then add garam masala and kewra or rose water if used.
Rest 10 minutes before serving so the gravy settles around the meat.

title: Nihari
description: A practical home-style Nihari recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 kg beef shank or boneless stew meat
3 tbsp whole-wheat flour mixed with 1/2 cup water
Julienned ginger, coriander, green chillies and lemon for serving
steps: Heat ghee or oil in a heavy pot and brown the beef shank pieces for 8 to 10 minutes.
Add ginger-garlic paste and nihari spices and cook for 2 minutes, stirring so the spices do not scorch.
Pour in enough hot water to cover the meat well. Bring to a boil, skim, then cover and simmer on the lowest heat for 4 to 6 hours until the meat is extremely tender.
Lift out the meat carefully. Strain the broth only if necessary, then return it to the pot.
Mix flour with cool water into a smooth slurry. Whisk it gradually into the simmering broth and cook for 15 to 20 minutes until lightly thick and silky.
Return the meat and simmer very gently for another 10 minutes without breaking the pieces.
Taste for salt and spice and finish with a spoonful of hot ghee if desired.
Serve piping hot with ginger, green chilli, coriander, lemon and naan.

title: Paya
description: A practical home-style Paya recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 4 cleaned goat or beef trotters, cut into pieces
Ginger, coriander, green chilli and lemon for garnish
steps: Rinse the cleaned trotters thoroughly. Blanch them in boiling water for 8 to 10 minutes, drain and rinse again to remove impurities.
Heat oil or ghee in a large pot and fry onion until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add whisked yogurt, coriander, cumin, chilli and turmeric and cook for 5 minutes until the masala is smooth and glossy.
Add the trotters and water. Bring to a boil, skim the surface and reduce to the gentlest simmer.
Cover and cook for 5 to 7 hours, or pressure-cook for 75 to 90 minutes, until the meat is tender and the broth is naturally gelatinous.
Remove loose bone fragments and simmer uncovered for 15 to 20 minutes to concentrate the broth.
Add garam masala and adjust salt. Keep the soup hot above 74°C (165°F) for serving.
Garnish with ginger, coriander, green chilli and lemon and serve with naan.

title: Haleem
description: A practical home-style Haleem recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: Fried onions, ginger, coriander, green chilli and lemon for garnish
steps: Soak the cracked wheat, barley and lentils for at least 2 hours. Rinse and cook them with plenty of water over low heat for 60 to 90 minutes until completely soft.
In a separate heavy pot, fry sliced onions until deep golden; reserve one-third for garnish.
Add ginger-garlic paste, meat, haleem masala, chilli and turmeric and sear for 8 minutes.
Add water, cover and simmer until the meat is very tender. Remove bones if present and shred the meat finely.
Blend or beat the cooked grain mixture until thick and smooth but not gluey.
Combine grains, shredded meat and meat gravy in a large pot. Cook over low heat for 30 to 45 minutes, stirring and beating from the bottom every few minutes.
Adjust with hot water to a slow-dropping consistency and add garam masala. The haleem should form long, cohesive strands when lifted.
Serve with fried onions, ginger, coriander, green chilli, lemon and a drizzle of ghee.

title: Chicken Qorma
description: A practical home-style Chicken Qorma recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Fry the sliced onions in oil or ghee until evenly deep golden, then remove and crush them finely.
Add whole spices to the same oil, followed by ginger-garlic paste, and cook for 1 minute.
Add chicken and fry for 6 to 8 minutes until the pieces are lightly coloured.
Whisk yogurt with chilli, coriander and turmeric. Add it gradually over low heat while stirring.
Return the crushed onions and cook the masala for 8 to 10 minutes until oil separates.
Add hot water, cover and simmer for 15 to 20 minutes until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F).
Finish with garam masala and kewra water if included and reduce to a rich, spoon-coating gravy.
Rest the chicken qorma for 5 minutes before serving so the fried-onion gravy settles around the meat.

title: Beef Qorma
description: A practical home-style Beef Qorma recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Fry the sliced onions in oil or ghee until deep golden, drain and grind them with a little water.
Add whole spices and ginger-garlic paste to the pot and cook for 1 minute.
Add beef pieces and sear over medium-high heat for 8 to 10 minutes.
Lower the heat and stir in whisked yogurt and ground spices gradually. Cook until the yogurt is absorbed.
Add the fried-onion paste and fry for 8 minutes until the masala darkens and oil separates.
Pour in hot water, cover and simmer for 75 to 100 minutes, or pressure-cook for 30 minutes, until the beef is tender.
Reduce the gravy, add garam masala and kewra if used and taste for salt.
Rest covered for 10 minutes before serving with naan.

title: Aloo Gosht
description: A practical home-style Aloo Gosht recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil in a heavy pot and brown the sliced onion for 8 minutes. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add the beef or mutton and sear for 8 to 10 minutes until lightly browned.
Add tomatoes, yogurt, chilli, turmeric, cumin and coriander and cook for 10 to 12 minutes until the masala thickens.
Add hot water, cover and simmer until the meat is nearly tender—about 50 to 80 minutes depending on the cut.
Add the potato pieces and enough water for the desired gravy. Cover and cook for 15 to 20 minutes until the potatoes are tender but not breaking.
Uncover and simmer for 5 minutes so the gravy thickens slightly around the meat and potatoes.
Add garam masala and fresh coriander and adjust salt.
Rest the aloo gosht for 5 minutes, then serve with roti or rice while the potatoes still hold their shape.

title: Daal Gosht
description: A practical home-style Daal Gosht recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the chana dal for 30 minutes, rinse and drain.
Heat oil and brown the sliced onion. Add ginger-garlic paste and the beef or mutton and sear for 8 minutes.
Add tomatoes, cumin, coriander, chilli and turmeric and cook until the tomatoes form a thick masala.
Add hot water, cover and simmer until the meat is about three-quarters tender.
Add the soaked dal and enough hot water to cover. Simmer for 30 to 40 minutes until both meat and dal are tender, stirring gently so some lentils remain whole.
Mash only a small spoonful of dal against the pot to thicken the gravy.
Season with salt, garam masala and lemon and simmer uncovered for 5 minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve hot.

title: Palak Gosht
description: A practical home-style Palak Gosht recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g spinach, mustard greens or mixed leafy greens
steps: Wash the spinach thoroughly, chop it and cook with only the water clinging to the leaves for 5 to 7 minutes. Blend or chop it coarsely.
Heat oil in a heavy pot and fry onion until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add mutton or beef and sear for 8 to 10 minutes.
Add tomatoes, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli and salt and cook until the masala is thick and oil separates.
Add hot water, cover and simmer until the meat is tender.
Stir in the cooked spinach and green chillies and simmer uncovered for 12 to 15 minutes so the flavours combine and excess water evaporates.
Finish with garam masala and a spoonful of ghee.
Rest for 5 minutes and serve with roti.

title: Kofta Curry
description: A practical home-style Kofta Curry recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 small onion, grated and squeezed dry
steps: Combine mince, grated squeezed onion, gram flour, half the ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, coriander, garam masala and salt.
Knead the mixture firmly by hand for 3 to 4 minutes until sticky, then shape into 16 smooth balls with lightly oiled hands. Chill for 20 minutes.
Heat oil in a wide pot and fry sliced onions until golden. Add the remaining ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, yogurt and ground spices and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until the masala thickens and oil separates.
Add hot water and bring the gravy to a gentle simmer. Slide in the chilled koftas one at a time without stirring.
Cover and simmer on low for 12 minutes. Shake the pot gently, then turn the koftas carefully and cook another 8 to 10 minutes.
Uncover and reduce the gravy to the desired consistency; the koftas should be cooked through but tender.
Finish with garam masala and coriander and rest 5 minutes before serving.

title: Chicken Handi
description: A practical home-style Chicken Handi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy handi or thick saucepan and cook the chopped onion until soft and lightly golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and chicken and fry over medium-high heat for 6 to 8 minutes.
Add blended tomatoes, chilli, cumin and coriander and cook for 8 minutes until the masala is thick.
Lower the heat and add whisked yogurt gradually, stirring continuously. Cover and simmer for 10 minutes.
Uncover and cook until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F) and the oil begins to separate.
Stir in cream, dried fenugreek and garam masala and cook over low heat for 3 minutes without boiling hard.
Add green chillies and adjust salt.
Rest covered for 5 minutes and serve with naan.

title: Boneless Handi
description: A practical home-style Boneless Handi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pan and cook the finely chopped onion for 6 to 8 minutes until pale golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and boneless chicken cubes and sear for 5 to 6 minutes.
Add blended tomatoes, cumin, coriander and chilli and cook over medium heat until the tomato moisture evaporates.
Whisk the yogurt smooth and add it in small amounts while stirring. Cover and cook for 8 to 10 minutes.
Uncover and cook until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F) and the masala clings to the cubes.
Lower the heat and stir in cream, dried fenugreek and garam masala for 2 to 3 minutes.
Add sliced green chillies and taste for salt; the sauce should be rich and thick, not runny.
Rest the boneless handi for 5 minutes so the cream, fenugreek, and tomato gravy finishes thickening before serving.

title: Achari Chicken
description: A practical home-style Achari Chicken recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix the chicken with yogurt, half the ginger-garlic paste, chilli, turmeric, coriander and salt and marinate for 30 minutes.
Heat oil in a heavy pot and cook sliced onion until golden.
Add the remaining ginger-garlic paste and the marinated chicken. Fry over medium-high heat for 7 to 8 minutes.
Add tomatoes and cook covered for 8 minutes until soft, then uncover and reduce the masala.
Toast the mixed pickle spices briefly, crush them coarsely and stir them into the chicken with green chillies.
Add a small amount of water, cover and simmer for 10 to 12 minutes until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F).
Uncover, add lemon juice and cook for 3 minutes until the oil separates and the tangy spice coats the chicken.
Rest for 5 minutes and serve with naan or paratha.

title: Butter Chicken
description: A practical home-style Butter Chicken recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Marinate the chicken in yogurt, half the ginger-garlic paste, chilli, turmeric and salt for at least 1 hour.
Sear the marinated chicken in a hot pan with a little oil for 6 to 8 minutes until browned; it need not be fully cooked. Set aside.
Melt half the butter in the same pan and cook onion until soft. Add remaining ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, cumin, coriander and chilli and simmer for 12 to 15 minutes until very soft. Blend until smooth and return to the pan.
Add the chicken and any juices and simmer gently for 8 to 10 minutes until it reaches 74°C (165°F).
Stir in cream, remaining butter, garam masala and crushed dried fenugreek over low heat.
Simmer for 2 minutes without boiling hard and adjust salt and sweetness if needed.
Serve the butter chicken hot with naan or rice while the butter-enriched sauce is smooth and glossy.

title: Mutton Rogan Josh
description: A practical home-style Mutton Rogan Josh recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil or ghee in a heavy pot and add whole spices. Stir for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add mutton and brown in batches over medium-high heat for 8 to 10 minutes.
Add ginger-garlic paste, chilli, fennel, ginger powder and coriander and cook for 2 minutes.
Lower the heat and add whisked yogurt gradually, stirring constantly until absorbed and the fat separates.
Add hot water, cover and simmer gently for 60 to 90 minutes until the mutton is fork-tender.
Uncover and reduce the gravy to a glossy, medium-thick consistency with a deep red colour from spices rather than excessive tomato.
Add garam masala and taste for salt.
Rest covered for 10 minutes before serving with rice or naan.

title: Chicken Tikka
description: A practical home-style Chicken Tikka recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp paprika or red chilli powder
steps: Score the chicken pieces deeply in several places so the marinade can penetrate.
Mix yogurt, lemon juice, ginger-garlic paste, chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, salt and oil until smooth.
Rub the marinade thoroughly into the chicken and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight.
Remove from the refrigerator 30 minutes before cooking. Heat a grill or oven to 230°C (450°F) and oil the grate or rack.
Arrange chicken in one layer and cook for 12 minutes, then turn and baste lightly with oil or butter.
Cook another 8 to 12 minutes until charred at the edges and the thickest part reaches 74°C (165°F).
Rest for 5 minutes so the juices settle.
Serve with lemon, onion and mint chutney.

title: Chicken Malai Tikka
description: A practical home-style Chicken Malai Tikka recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp paprika or red chilli powder
steps: Cut the chicken into even cubes and pat dry. Mix cream, yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, white pepper, cardamom, cheese or cornflour and salt.
Coat the chicken thoroughly and marinate in the refrigerator for 3 to 6 hours.
Thread the pieces onto skewers with small gaps so heat can circulate.
Heat a grill or oven to 220°C (425°F) and lightly oil the rack.
Grill for 8 to 10 minutes, turn and brush with melted butter or cream.
Cook for another 6 to 8 minutes until lightly charred and the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F), avoiding excessive browning that would dry the creamy coating.
Rest the skewers for 4 minutes.
Serve hot with mint chutney and lemon.

title: Beef Seekh Kebab
description: A practical home-style Beef Seekh Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp paprika or red chilli powder
steps: Combine beef mince, finely chopped onion, green chilli, coriander, ginger-garlic paste, cumin, coriander, garam masala, chilli and salt.
Knead forcefully for 5 minutes until the mince becomes sticky and holds together. Chill for 30 minutes.
Wet or oil your hands and press the mince evenly around flat metal skewers, making long kebabs without thick centres.
Heat a charcoal or gas grill to medium-high, about 220°C (425°F), and oil the grate.
Grill the kebabs for 3 to 4 minutes per side, turning carefully as the exterior firms.
Continue cooking until browned with light char and the centre reaches at least 71°C (160°F).
Rest for 3 minutes before sliding from the skewers.
Serve with onion rings, lemon and chutney.

title: Chicken Seekh Kebab
description: A practical home-style Chicken Seekh Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp paprika or red chilli powder
steps: Mix chicken mince with squeezed onion, ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, coriander, cumin, garam masala, chilli, gram flour and salt.
Knead for 4 to 5 minutes until tacky, then refrigerate for 30 minutes.
With wet hands, shape the mixture around skewers in even cylinders, pressing firmly so no cracks remain.
Heat a grill to medium-high and brush the kebabs lightly with oil.
Cook for 3 to 4 minutes per side, turning only after the first side has set.
Grill until lightly charred and the centre reaches 74°C (165°F).
Rest for 3 minutes and brush with a little melted butter if desired.
Serve immediately with mint chutney.

title: Reshmi Kebab
description: A practical home-style Reshmi Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 small onion, grated and squeezed dry
steps: Blend onion, green chilli, coriander, ginger-garlic paste and cream into a smooth paste.
Mix the paste with chicken mince, grated cheese, white pepper, cardamom, cornflour and salt.
Knead for 4 minutes until smooth and sticky, then chill for 45 minutes.
Shape the mixture around oiled skewers with wet hands, keeping the kebabs narrow and even.
Grill over medium heat, turning every 2 to 3 minutes, so the delicate mixture sets without burning.
Brush lightly with butter and continue until pale golden with a few charred spots and an internal temperature of 74°C (165°F).
Rest the grilled kebabs for 3 minutes so the juices settle and the delicate mince firms before serving.
Serve with a mild mint-yogurt chutney and lemon.

title: Chapli Kebab
description: A practical home-style Chapli Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 onion, finely chopped and squeezed dry
steps: Mix the coarsely ground beef or mutton with crushed coriander and cumin, chilli, salt, pomegranate seeds, chopped tomato, onion, green chilli, coriander and egg.
Add cornmeal or gram flour and knead gently for 2 minutes; do not overwork into a smooth paste.
Rest the mixture for 20 to 30 minutes so the flour absorbs moisture.
Wet your hands and shape broad, thin patties about 1 cm thick, pressing a tomato slice on top if desired.
Heat 5 mm oil or rendered fat in a heavy skillet over medium-high heat to about 175°C (350°F).
Fry for 3 to 4 minutes on the first side until deeply browned, then turn once and cook another 3 minutes.
Ensure the centre reaches at least 71°C (160°F), then drain briefly.
Serve hot with naan, chutney and onion.

title: Shami Kebab
description: A practical home-style Shami Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Place meat, chana dal, onion, ginger, garlic, whole spices, chilli, salt and enough water to cover in a pot.
Simmer uncovered until the meat and dal are completely tender and all water has evaporated, about 45 to 60 minutes.
Cool slightly, remove any whole hard spices and grind to a smooth, firm mixture.
Knead in chopped coriander, mint, green chilli, garam masala and egg only as needed for binding.
Shape into flat round patties and chill for 20 minutes.
Heat a thin layer of oil over medium heat. Dip patties lightly in beaten egg if preferred and fry for 2 to 3 minutes per side.
Cook until evenly golden and hot through without drying the centre.
Drain and serve with chutney or use in burgers.

title: Gola Kebab
description: A practical home-style Gola Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix mince with fried-onion powder, ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, coriander, cumin, chilli, garam masala, raw papaya paste and salt.
Knead for 5 to 6 minutes until very smooth and sticky, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours.
Divide into portions and shape each around a skewer into a compact oval or cylinder.
Heat a grill to medium-high and brush the kebabs with oil.
Cook for 3 to 4 minutes per side, turning gently until evenly browned and lightly charred.
Continue until the centre reaches at least 71°C (160°F).
Rest for 3 minutes and brush with melted butter if desired.
Serve with paratha, chutney and sliced onion.

title: Bihari Kebab
description: A practical home-style Bihari Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Slice the beef across the grain into very thin strips and lightly pound any thick areas.
Mix yogurt, fried-onion paste, ginger-garlic paste, mustard oil, raw papaya, chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala and salt.
Massage the marinade into the beef for 3 minutes and refrigerate for 6 to 12 hours.
Thread the strips lengthwise onto skewers, folding them so they do not hang loose.
Grill over hot charcoal or under a 230°C (450°F) broiler for 4 to 5 minutes per side.
Baste with oil or butter and continue cooking until browned, smoky, and tender; beef should reach at least 63°C (145°F) after resting, or a higher doneness selected for the cut.
Rest the kebabs for 5 minutes so the tenderised beef retains its juices when removed from the skewers.
Serve with paratha, onion and lemon.

title: Dhaga Kebab
description: A practical home-style Dhaga Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Combine mince with onion, ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, coriander, cumin, garam masala, chilli, gram flour and salt.
Knead for 5 minutes until tacky and chill for 30 minutes.
Shape the mince evenly around skewers, then wrap clean cotton thread spirally around each kebab to support it during cooking.
Grill over medium-high heat, turning every 2 to 3 minutes until the surface is set and browned.
Brush lightly with oil and continue until the kebabs reach at least 71°C (160°F) internally.
Rest for 3 minutes, then unwind and remove every piece of thread carefully.
Slide the kebabs from the skewers without breaking them.
Serve hot with chutney and naan.

title: Mutton Chops
description: A practical home-style Mutton Chops recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Trim excess fat from the chops and lightly flatten the meat near the bone for even cooking.
Mix yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, lemon, chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala and salt and coat the chops thoroughly.
Marinate in the refrigerator for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight.
Bring to room temperature for 25 minutes and heat a grill or cast-iron pan to medium-high.
Shake off excess marinade and sear the chops for 3 to 4 minutes per side.
Lower the heat and cook another 2 to 4 minutes per side, depending on thickness, until tender and at least 63°C (145°F) internally.
Rest the mutton chops for 5 minutes before serving so their juices remain in the meat.
Finish with lemon and chaat masala if desired.

title: Tandoori Chicken
description: A practical home-style Tandoori Chicken recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp paprika or red chilli powder
steps: Remove the skin and cut deep slashes into the chicken legs and thighs.
Rub first with lemon juice and salt and rest for 20 minutes.
Mix yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric and oil and press the marinade into every cut.
Refrigerate for 6 to 12 hours, then remove 30 minutes before cooking.
Heat the oven to 240°C (465°F) with a rack over a tray, or prepare a very hot grill.
Roast or grill for 20 minutes, turn and baste with oil or butter, then cook another 15 to 20 minutes until charred and 74°C (165°F) at the bone.
Broil for 2 minutes if more surface colour is needed, watching closely.
Rest 5 minutes and serve with lemon and onion.

title: Fish Tikka
description: A practical home-style Fish Tikka recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g firm white fish fillets, cut into chunks
steps: Cut firm fish into equal cubes and pat completely dry.
Mix yogurt, lemon juice, ginger-garlic paste, chilli, cumin, coriander, turmeric, gram flour and salt.
Coat the fish gently and marinate for only 30 to 45 minutes; longer acidic marination can make it mushy.
Thread onto skewers and brush lightly with oil.
Grill or broil at 230°C (450°F) for 4 to 5 minutes on the first side.
Turn carefully and cook another 3 to 5 minutes until lightly charred and the fish flakes at about 63°C (145°F).
Rest the fish for 2 minutes off the heat, allowing carryover cooking to finish without drying the cubes.
Serve immediately with lemon and mint chutney.

title: Behari Boti
description: A practical home-style Behari Boti recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Slice the beef into thin bite-size strips across the grain and lightly pound thicker pieces.
Mix yogurt, fried-onion paste, ginger-garlic paste, raw papaya, mustard oil, chilli, cumin, coriander and garam masala.
Massage the marinade into the beef and refrigerate for at least 6 hours.
Thread the pieces tightly onto skewers so they support one another.
Grill over high heat for 4 minutes, turn and baste with oil.
Cook another 4 to 6 minutes until charred at the edges and tender, avoiding prolonged cooking that toughens the thin meat.
Rest for 4 minutes and sprinkle with lemon.
Serve with paratha and onion.

title: Chicken Boti
description: A practical home-style Chicken Boti recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp paprika or red chilli powder
steps: Cut boneless chicken into equal cubes and pat dry.
Mix yogurt, lemon, ginger-garlic paste, chilli, cumin, coriander, garam masala and salt.
Coat the chicken and marinate for 2 to 4 hours in the refrigerator.
Thread onto skewers with small spaces between pieces and brush lightly with oil.
Grill over medium-high heat for 5 minutes, turn and baste.
Cook another 5 to 7 minutes until lightly charred and the centre reaches 74°C (165°F).
Rest the chicken boti for 3 minutes before removing it from the skewers so the juices redistribute.
Serve with mint chutney, lemon and naan.

title: Bun Kebab
description: A practical home-style Bun Kebab recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Prepare or reheat the shami-style patties and keep them warm.
Slice the buns and toast the cut sides on a lightly buttered griddle until golden.
Beat the egg with a pinch of salt. Pour a thin layer on the griddle and place a kebab patty on it.
Fold the setting egg around the patty and cook for 1 to 2 minutes per side until the egg is fully set.
Spread chutney on both bun halves and add sliced onion, tomato and cucumber if used.
Place the egg-wrapped kebab on the lower bun and add extra chutney or ketchup to taste.
Close the bun and press lightly on the griddle for 30 seconds.
Serve immediately while the bun is crisp and the centre is hot.

title: Anda Shami Burger
description: A practical home-style Anda Shami Burger recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Warm the shami kebab patties in a skillet with a small amount of oil until browned on both sides.
Beat the eggs with salt and pepper. Pour one portion into the skillet and place a hot shami patty in the centre.
Fold the egg around the patty and cook until no liquid egg remains.
Butter and toast the burger buns cut-side down until golden.
Spread mint chutney and ketchup or chilli sauce over the buns.
Add the egg-shami patty, onion, tomato and cucumber or cabbage.
Close and press gently on the hot pan for 30 to 45 seconds.
Serve immediately with extra chutney.

title: Chicken Roll
description: A practical home-style Chicken Roll recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Marinate chicken strips with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, chilli, cumin, lemon and salt for at least 30 minutes.
Cook the chicken in a hot skillet with oil for 6 to 8 minutes until browned, dry and 74°C (165°F).
Warm the parathas on a griddle until flexible and lightly crisp at the edges.
Spread mint-yogurt chutney down the centre of each paratha.
Add hot chicken, sliced onion, cabbage or cucumber and a squeeze of lemon.
Fold the bottom edge up, roll tightly and wrap the lower half in paper or foil.
Return seam-side down to the griddle for 30 seconds if a crisper roll is desired.
Serve immediately before the paratha softens.

title: Beef Roll
description: A practical home-style Beef Roll recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Marinate thin beef strips with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, raw papaya if used, chilli, cumin, coriander and salt for 2 to 4 hours.
Heat oil in a wide pan and sear the beef over high heat for 5 to 7 minutes until browned and tender; cook in batches if needed.
Reduce any remaining marinade until it clings to the beef and no raw liquid remains.
Warm the parathas on a griddle until pliable and lightly crisp.
Spread chutney over each paratha and add hot beef, onion, cucumber or cabbage and lemon.
Fold in the lower edge and roll tightly around the filling.
Toast seam-side down for 30 seconds and wrap the base in paper.
Serve at once with extra chutney.

title: Club Sandwich
description: A practical home-style Club Sandwich recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Season the chicken and cook it in a skillet until it reaches 74°C (165°F), then cool slightly and slice or shred.
Toast the bread slices evenly until golden but still flexible enough to cut cleanly.
Mix mayonnaise with black pepper and a little mustard if included.
Spread mayonnaise on one toast slice and layer lettuce, chicken, tomato and cucumber.
Place a second toast slice on top, spread both sides lightly with mayonnaise and add egg, cheese and another layer of vegetables.
Finish with the third toast slice and press gently without crushing the filling.
Secure with four toothpicks and cut diagonally into quarters with a serrated knife.
Serve immediately with fries or crisps.

title: Chana Chaat
description: A practical home-style Chana Chaat recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: If using dried chickpeas, soak overnight and simmer in fresh water until tender but not broken; drain and cool completely.
Boil the potato cubes in salted water until just tender, drain and cool so they hold their shape.
Whisk tamarind chutney, lemon juice, chaat masala, roasted cumin, chilli and salt in a large bowl.
Add chickpeas, potatoes, finely chopped onion, tomato and green chilli.
Fold gently for 1 to 2 minutes so the seasoning coats everything without crushing the chickpeas.
Rest for 10 minutes at room temperature to let the chickpeas absorb the tangy dressing.
Stir in coriander immediately before serving and adjust lemon, salt and chilli.
Serve at room temperature; add papri only at the last moment if using so it stays crisp.

title: Fruit Chaat
description: A practical home-style Fruit Chaat recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Wash, peel and cut the fruits into similar bite-size pieces. Keep bananas and apples until last to reduce browning.
Mix orange juice or lemon juice with sugar, chaat masala, black salt, roasted cumin and a pinch of chilli until the sugar dissolves.
Place firm fruits such as apple, pear and guava in a bowl and toss with half the dressing.
Add softer fruits such as banana, mango, grapes and orange segments.
Fold gently with a broad spoon so the fruit stays intact and every piece is lightly seasoned.
Taste and add more black salt or citrus; the dressing should be sweet, tangy and slightly savoury.
Chill for 20 to 30 minutes, but not for several hours because the fruit releases too much juice.
Serve cold with pomegranate seeds or mint on top.

title: Dahi Bhallay
description: A practical home-style Dahi Bhallay recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 12 prepared lentil dumplings, soaked and squeezed
steps: Soak the urad or moong dal for 4 to 6 hours, drain and grind with very little water into a thick, smooth batter.
Beat the batter vigorously for 4 to 5 minutes until lighter. Test a small drop in water; it should float.
Heat frying oil to 170°C (340°F). Drop small spoonfuls of batter and fry for 4 to 5 minutes, turning until pale golden and cooked through.
Drain, then soak the hot bhallay in warm lightly salted water for 15 to 20 minutes.
Press each bhalla gently between the palms to remove water without flattening it completely.
Whisk yogurt with sugar and salt until smooth and pour a layer into the serving dish. Arrange the bhallay and cover with remaining yogurt.
Drizzle with tamarind and mint chutneys and sprinkle chaat masala, roasted cumin and chilli.
Chill for 30 minutes and garnish with coriander or sev just before serving.

title: Papri Chaat
description: A practical home-style Papri Chaat recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Boil the potatoes until just tender, cool and cut into small cubes. Rinse and drain the cooked chickpeas.
Whisk yogurt with a little sugar and salt until smooth and pourable.
Arrange half the papri in a shallow serving dish and scatter over potatoes, chickpeas, onion and tomato.
Spoon yogurt across the surface, followed by tamarind chutney and green chutney.
Add the remaining papri, breaking a few pieces to create different textures.
Sprinkle chaat masala, roasted cumin, chilli and black salt evenly.
Finish with sev, coriander and pomegranate seeds if included.
Serve immediately before the papri absorbs the yogurt and loses its crunch.

title: Gol Gappay
description: A practical home-style Gol Gappay recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix semolina, flour and salt, then add water gradually and knead for 8 to 10 minutes into a firm, smooth dough.
Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 30 minutes. Roll very thin and cut into small circles, keeping them covered so they do not dry.
Heat oil to 190°C (375°F). Slide in a few circles, press each lightly under the oil until it puffs, then fry until crisp and pale golden.
Drain completely and cool before storing airtight; discard puris that remain flat or soft.
For the pani, blend mint, coriander, green chilli, ginger, tamarind, roasted cumin, black salt and water, then strain and chill for at least 1 hour.
Mix boiled potato and chickpeas with chaat masala and salt for the filling.
Crack a small opening in each puri, add a teaspoon of filling and, if desired, a little tamarind chutney.
Fill with cold spiced pani and eat immediately in one bite.

title: Aloo Chaat
description: A practical home-style Aloo Chaat recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Boil the potatoes until just tender, cool completely and cut into 2 cm cubes.
Heat oil in a wide skillet over medium-high heat and fry the potato cubes for 8 to 10 minutes until crisp and golden on several sides.
Lower the heat and add roasted cumin, chilli, chaat masala and salt; toss for 30 seconds.
Transfer to a bowl and add tamarind chutney and lemon juice while the potatoes are still warm.
Fold in chopped onion, tomato, green chilli and coriander.
Taste for a balance of sour, spicy and salty and adjust with black salt or lemon.
Add sev or crushed papri only at serving time if included.
Serve warm for crisp potatoes or at room temperature after a 10-minute rest for stronger seasoning.

title: Chana Chaat Masala
description: A practical home-style Chana Chaat Masala recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Cook soaked chickpeas until tender, drain well and keep slightly warm so they absorb the spice dressing.
Dry-roast cumin and coriander seeds until fragrant, cool and grind with black pepper, chilli, black salt and dried mango powder.
Whisk tamarind pulp, lemon juice and 2 to 3 teaspoons of the fresh chaat masala in a serving bowl.
Add warm chickpeas, boiled potato, onion, tomato and green chilli.
Toss thoroughly for 2 minutes, lightly crushing a few chickpeas against the bowl to help the dressing cling.
Rest for 15 minutes, then taste and add more spice blend only as needed.
Fold in coriander and mint.
Serve with extra house chaat masala on the side.

title: Samosa Chaat
description: A practical home-style Samosa Chaat recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Warm the samosas in a 190°C (375°F) oven for 8 to 10 minutes so the pastry becomes crisp.
Heat the cooked chickpea curry until thick and spoonable rather than watery.
Whisk yogurt with sugar and salt until smooth.
Break one or two hot samosas into large pieces in each shallow bowl.
Spoon hot chickpeas over the samosa pieces, leaving some pastry exposed.
Add yogurt, tamarind chutney and green chutney in separate ribbons.
Sprinkle chaat masala, roasted cumin, chilli, onion and coriander.
Finish with sev and serve immediately while the centre is hot and some pastry remains crisp.

title: Kachori Chaat
description: A practical home-style Kachori Chaat recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Reheat the kachoris in a 180°C (350°F) oven for 8 minutes until crisp; avoid microwaving because it softens the shell.
Warm the chickpea or potato curry and keep it thick enough to coat a spoon.
Whisk yogurt with a pinch of sugar and salt.
Crack the top of each kachori and place it in a shallow bowl.
Fill and surround it with warm curry, then spoon over yogurt.
Drizzle tamarind chutney and mint chutney and add chopped onion and tomato.
Sprinkle chaat masala, cumin, chilli and sev.
Serve immediately so the kachori gives both crisp shell and softened, chutney-soaked edges.

title: Tikki Chaat
description: A practical home-style Tikki Chaat recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mash the boiled potatoes while warm, mix with salt, chilli, cumin and a little cornflour or breadcrumbs and cool completely.
Shape into firm flat patties and chill for 20 minutes so they do not crack.
Heat a thin layer of oil over medium heat and fry the tikkis for 4 to 5 minutes per side until deeply crisp and golden.
Place two hot tikkis in each serving bowl and press them lightly with the back of a spoon.
Spoon warm chickpeas over and around them.
Add whisked yogurt, tamarind chutney and green chutney.
Sprinkle chaat masala, roasted cumin, onion, coriander and sev.
Serve at once while the tikki crust is crisp.

title: Saag
description: A practical home-style Saag recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g spinach, mustard greens or mixed leafy greens
steps: Pick over the mixed greens, remove tough stems and wash repeatedly until no grit remains. Chop roughly.
Place the greens, green chillies, ginger and a small amount of water in a heavy pot. Cover and cook over low heat for 35 to 45 minutes until very soft.
Mash with a wooden churner or blend briefly to a coarse texture; saag should not become a completely smooth purée.
Stir in maize flour gradually and simmer uncovered for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring often, until thick.
In a separate pan, heat ghee and fry sliced garlic and onion until golden.
Add chilli and cumin to the tempering for 20 seconds, then pour it over the saag.
Simmer together for 5 minutes and adjust salt.
Serve hot with a knob of butter and makki ki roti.

title: Makki Ki Roti
description: A practical home-style Makki Ki Roti recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 tbsp ghee or butter for cooking
steps: Combine maize flour and salt in a bowl. Add warm water gradually and knead for 3 to 4 minutes into a soft dough that holds when pressed.
Rest covered for 10 minutes so the flour hydrates. Keep extra warm water nearby because maize dough dries quickly.
Divide into balls. Pat one ball between damp palms or press it between two sheets of plastic into a round about 4 to 5 mm thick.
Peel away the top sheet and transfer carefully to a medium-hot tawa.
Cook for 2 to 3 minutes until the edges dry, then turn with a wide spatula.
Cook the second side for 2 minutes, brush lightly with ghee and turn once more until brown spots appear and the centre is cooked.
Keep wrapped in a clean towel while cooking the remaining rotis.
Serve hot with saag, butter and jaggery.

title: Sarson Ka Saag
description: A practical home-style Sarson Ka Saag recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g spinach, mustard greens or mixed leafy greens
steps: Wash mustard greens, spinach and bathua thoroughly, discard coarse stems and chop the leaves.
Cook the greens with green chillies, ginger and 1 cup water over low heat for 45 to 60 minutes until completely tender.
Mash traditionally with a wooden churner or pulse briefly; leave a slightly coarse texture.
Whisk maize flour with a little cool water and stir it into the greens.
Simmer uncovered for 20 minutes, stirring from the bottom, until thick and the raw flour taste disappears.
Heat ghee in a small pan and fry onion and garlic until deep golden; add dried chilli and cumin for 20 seconds.
Pour the tempering into the saag, simmer 5 minutes and adjust salt.
Serve with butter, makki ki roti and sliced ginger.

title: Bhindi Masala
description: A practical home-style Bhindi Masala recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 600 g okra, washed, dried and sliced
1/2 tsp amchur or 1 tbsp lemon juice
steps: Wash the okra and dry it completely before trimming and cutting; any moisture will make it slimy.
Heat half the oil in a wide pan over medium-high heat. Fry the okra in one or two batches for 8 to 10 minutes until lightly browned and no longer sticky; remove.
Add the remaining oil and cook onion for 6 to 8 minutes until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste for 45 seconds, then tomatoes, cumin, coriander, chilli, turmeric and salt.
Cook the masala for 7 to 9 minutes until thick and oil appears at the edges.
Return the okra and fold gently to coat it without breaking the pieces.
Cook uncovered over low heat for 6 to 8 minutes and finish with garam masala or amchur.
Serve hot once the okra is tender but still holds its shape.

title: Baingan Bharta
description: A practical home-style Baingan Bharta recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Pierce the eggplants and roast directly over a gas flame, charcoal or under a very hot broiler, turning until the skin is blackened and the flesh collapses.
Place in a covered bowl for 10 minutes, peel away the burnt skin and discard any heavily charred fragments.
Drain excess liquid and mash the smoky flesh coarsely.
Heat oil and cook onion until golden. Add ginger, garlic and green chilli and stir for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, chilli, turmeric, cumin and salt and cook until thick and glossy.
Add the mashed eggplant and cook uncovered for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring and pressing until well combined.
Finish with coriander and garam masala and cook for 2 minutes.
Serve hot with roti, preserving the smoky flavour.

title: Mixed Vegetable Curry
description: A practical home-style Mixed Vegetable Curry recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Cut potatoes, carrots, cauliflower, beans and other vegetables into even pieces, keeping quick-cooking vegetables separate.
Heat oil in a heavy pan and fry onion until golden. Add cumin and ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, coriander, chilli, turmeric and salt and cook until the masala thickens.
Add potatoes and carrots first and fry in the masala for 3 minutes.
Add 1 cup water, cover and cook for 8 minutes, then add cauliflower, beans and peas.
Cover and simmer for another 8 to 12 minutes until all vegetables are tender but not mushy.
Uncover and reduce excess liquid, then add garam masala and coriander.
Rest the mixed vegetable curry for 5 minutes, then serve with roti or rice while the vegetables remain distinct.

title: Chana Masala
description: A practical home-style Chana Masala recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak dried chickpeas overnight, drain and simmer in fresh water until tender; reserve 1 cup cooking liquid.
Heat oil in a heavy pan and fry onion until deep golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chilli and cook for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, cumin, coriander, chilli, turmeric and chana masala and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until oil separates.
Add chickpeas and reserved liquid and simmer uncovered for 15 to 20 minutes.
Mash a small spoonful of chickpeas against the side of the pan to thicken the gravy.
Add tamarind or lemon and garam masala and adjust salt.
Garnish with coriander and ginger and serve hot.

title: Dal Mash
description: A practical home-style Dal Mash recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the split urad dal and soak for 1 to 2 hours, then drain.
Place in a pot with water, turmeric and salt and simmer gently for 25 to 35 minutes until tender but many grains remain distinct.
Drain excess water if necessary; dal mash is usually thick rather than soupy.
Heat ghee or oil in a frying pan and brown sliced onion.
Add garlic, cumin, dried chilli and chilli flakes and fry for 30 seconds.
Pour the hot tempering over the dal and fold gently.
Simmer together for 5 minutes, then add garam masala, green chilli and coriander.
Serve with lemon and naan or roti.

title: Dal Chana
description: A practical home-style Dal Chana recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse chana dal and soak for 30 to 60 minutes.
Combine dal, water, turmeric and salt and simmer for 30 to 40 minutes until tender but not completely dissolved.
In a separate pan, heat oil and fry onion until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, then tomatoes, cumin, coriander and chilli and cook until the masala is thick.
Stir the masala into the cooked dal and simmer for 8 to 10 minutes, adding hot water for the desired consistency.
Heat a little ghee with sliced garlic, cumin and dried chilli until sizzling.
Pour the tempering over the dal and cover for 2 minutes to trap the aroma.
Finish with coriander and lemon and serve hot.

title: Dal Tarka
description: A practical home-style Dal Tarka recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the lentils and cook with turmeric, salt and water until completely tender, 25 to 40 minutes depending on the lentil.
Whisk or mash lightly to make a creamy base while leaving some texture.
Add hot water if needed and simmer over low heat for 5 minutes.
For the first tarka, heat oil or ghee and fry onion until golden. Add tomato, ginger and green chilli and cook until soft; stir this into the dal.
In a small pan, heat ghee until hot and add sliced garlic, cumin seeds and dried red chillies.
Cook for 20 to 30 seconds until the garlic is golden and immediately pour over the dal.
Cover for 2 minutes, then add coriander and lemon.
Serve while the tempering is fragrant and sizzling.

title: Kadhi Pakora
description: A practical home-style Kadhi Pakora recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk yogurt, gram flour, turmeric, chilli, salt and water for 2 minutes until no lumps remain.
Heat oil in a deep pot, add fenugreek seeds, cumin, curry leaves and dried chilli and let them sizzle.
Pour in the yogurt mixture while stirring. Bring slowly to a boil, then simmer uncovered for 35 to 45 minutes, stirring every few minutes.
For the pakoras, mix gram flour, onion, chilli, coriander, spices and enough water to make a thick dropping batter. Rest 10 minutes.
Heat frying oil to 175°C (350°F) and fry small pakoras for 4 to 5 minutes until golden and cooked through.
Add the drained pakoras to the simmering kadhi and cook for 8 to 10 minutes so they soften but do not disintegrate.
Make a final tempering of ghee, garlic, cumin and dried chilli and pour it over the kadhi.
Rest 10 minutes and serve with steamed rice.

title: Lahori Channay
description: A practical home-style Lahori Channay recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak chickpeas overnight. Simmer with water, salt and tea bag or black cardamom if included until very tender; reserve the cooking liquid.
Heat oil and fry onion until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, chilli, cumin, coriander, anardana and chana masala and cook until thick.
Add the chickpeas and enough reserved liquid for a generous gravy.
Simmer uncovered for 20 to 30 minutes, stirring and crushing a small portion of chickpeas to thicken.
Add baking soda only if specifically needed for stubborn chickpeas, and in a very small amount.
Finish with garam masala, green chilli, ginger and lemon.
Serve hot with kulcha or puri.

title: Murgh Cholay
description: A practical home-style Murgh Cholay recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak and cook the chickpeas until tender, reserving their liquid.
Heat oil in a heavy pot and fry onion until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and chicken and sear for 6 to 8 minutes.
Add tomatoes, yogurt, cumin, coriander, chilli and turmeric and cook until the masala thickens.
Add cooked chickpeas and 2 cups reserved liquid or hot water.
Cover and simmer for 15 minutes until the chicken reaches 74°C (165°F).
Uncover and simmer for another 8 minutes, crushing a few chickpeas to thicken the gravy.
Add garam masala, green chilli, ginger and coriander.
Rest 5 minutes and serve with naan or kulcha.

title: Sajji
description: A practical home-style Sajji recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 cups cooked rice for optional stuffing
steps: Clean and pat the whole chicken or small lamb carcass dry. Make shallow cuts in thick areas without tearing the skin excessively.
Mix salt, lemon juice and a small amount of crushed black pepper; traditional sajji uses restrained seasoning.
Rub inside and outside and marinate refrigerated for 4 to 8 hours.
Stuff the cavity loosely with prepared rice only if making stuffed sajji, leaving room for expansion, then secure the opening.
Skewer securely and roast beside charcoal or in a 190°C (375°F) oven, turning regularly.
Cook chicken for about 60 to 75 minutes or lamb much longer, basting lightly with fat, until the thickest meat is tender and safely cooked.
For chicken, verify at least 74°C (165°F) in the thigh without touching bone and rest for 15 minutes.
Serve whole with rice, lemon and the roasting juices.

title: Dampukht
