description: A practical home-style Dampukht recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: Dough made from 1 cup flour and water for sealing
steps: Mix mutton with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, browned onion, cumin, coriander, black pepper, garam masala, oil and salt. Marinate for at least 2 hours.
Transfer the meat and marinade to a heavy, tight-lidded pot and add only the measured water.
Place parchment or foil under the lid, then seal the rim completely with a rope of flour-and-water dough.
Set the pot over medium heat for 10 minutes until steam builds inside.
Reduce to the lowest heat and cook undisturbed for 1 1/2 to 2 hours; place a heat diffuser beneath the pot to prevent scorching.
Turn off the heat and rest sealed for 15 minutes before carefully breaking the dough seal away from your face.
Check the mutton is fork-tender. If excess liquid remains, reduce briefly uncovered without drying the meat.
Serve immediately with the concentrated sealed-pot juices.

title: Landi
description: A practical home-style Landi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 kg dried lamb or beef pieces, soaked and rinsed
steps: Rinse the dried meat briefly, then soak it in cool water for 2 to 4 hours, changing the water once to remove excess salt.
Drain and place the meat in a pot with fresh water. Bring to a boil, skim and simmer for 45 to 60 minutes to begin softening.
Heat oil in another heavy pot and cook onion until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and stir for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes, coriander, turmeric and black pepper and cook until a thick masala forms.
Add the partially cooked landi and enough of its strained broth to cover halfway.
Cover and simmer gently for 60 to 90 minutes until tender, adding hot broth as needed.
Uncover and reduce the gravy. Taste before adding salt because dried meat can remain strongly seasoned.
Serve the Landi hot with naan or rice, spooning the concentrated meat juices over each portion.

title: Mutton Kunna
description: A practical home-style Mutton Kunna recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 tbsp whole-wheat flour mixed with 1/3 cup water
steps: Heat ghee in a heavy clay pot or thick saucepan and brown the mutton in batches.
Add ginger-garlic paste, chilli, coriander, cumin and salt and fry for 2 minutes.
Add browned-onion paste or finely fried onions and enough hot water to cover the meat.
Cover and simmer on the lowest heat for 2 to 3 hours until the mutton is exceptionally tender.
Mix flour with cool water into a smooth slurry and stir it into the simmering gravy gradually.
Cook for 15 to 20 minutes until the gravy is glossy and lightly thickened, stirring from the bottom.
Add garam masala and crushed black pepper and adjust salt.
Rest 10 minutes and serve with naan, ginger and green chilli.

title: Namkeen Gosht
description: A practical home-style Namkeen Gosht recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Cut the mutton into large bone-in pieces and pat dry.
Heat the fat or oil in a heavy pot and sear the meat over medium-high heat for 8 to 10 minutes.
Add salt, crushed black pepper, garlic and green chillies; keep the seasoning simple.
Add a small amount of hot water, cover tightly and reduce to very low heat.
Cook for 60 to 90 minutes, stirring every 20 minutes and adding only a splash of water if the pot dries.
When the meat is tender, uncover and cook over medium-high heat until the remaining liquid reduces and the fat coats the pieces.
Taste carefully for salt and add no heavy spice mixture.
Rest 5 minutes and serve with naan.

title: Aloo Keema
description: A practical home-style Aloo Keema recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil in a wide pot and fry onion until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add minced beef or mutton and cook over medium-high heat for 8 to 10 minutes, breaking up all large clumps.
Add tomatoes, cumin, coriander, chilli, turmeric and salt and cook until the masala is thick and the mince looks glossy.
Add potato cubes and stir for 3 minutes so they are coated in the spices.
Pour in 1 to 1 1/2 cups hot water, cover and simmer for 15 to 20 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Uncover and cook until excess liquid evaporates and the mince separates rather than remaining soupy.
Add garam masala, green chilli and coriander.
Rest 5 minutes and serve with roti.

title: Keema Matar
description: A practical home-style Keema Matar recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil and cook onion until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste and stir for 1 minute.
Add mince and fry over medium-high heat for 8 minutes, breaking it into fine grains.
Add tomatoes, chilli, turmeric, cumin, coriander and salt and cook until oil separates.
Add peas and 1 cup hot water and bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook over low heat for 12 to 15 minutes until the peas are tender and the mince is fully cooked.
Uncover and cook over medium heat until the gravy reduces to the preferred consistency.
Add garam masala, green chillies and coriander.
Serve hot with roti, naan or rice.

title: Kheer
description: A practical home-style Kheer recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/2 cup basmati rice, washed and soaked 20 minutes
steps: Rinse the rice and soak for 30 minutes. Drain and crush the grains lightly between the fingers for a creamier texture.
Bring the milk to a boil in a heavy wide pot, then reduce to the lowest steady simmer.
Add the rice and cook for 45 to 60 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes and scraping the bottom and sides.
When the rice is completely soft and the milk has reduced by about one-third, add sugar and cardamom.
Continue cooking for 10 to 15 minutes until thick enough to coat a spoon; remember it will thicken further when cold.
Stir in nuts and rose or kewra water only during the final 2 minutes.
Cool for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent a thick skin, then refrigerate for at least 3 hours.
Serve chilled or warm with extra nuts.

title: Rice Kheer
description: A practical home-style Rice Kheer recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/2 cup basmati rice, washed and soaked 20 minutes
steps: Wash the basmati rice, soak for 20 minutes and drain. Grind or crush half the rice coarsely while leaving the rest whole.
Heat milk in a heavy-bottomed pot until it reaches a gentle boil.
Add both textures of rice and simmer on low for 50 to 65 minutes, stirring frequently so the milk does not scorch.
Press the softened whole grains lightly against the side of the pot to release starch.
Add sugar, cardamom and saffron and cook for another 10 minutes.
Fold in almonds, pistachios and raisins and simmer for 2 minutes.
Remove while slightly looser than the desired final consistency and add rose water if used.
Cool and chill before serving.

title: Sheer Khurma
description: A practical home-style Sheer Khurma recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the dried dates in warm milk or water for 20 minutes, then slice thinly.
Heat ghee in a heavy pot and fry almonds, pistachios, raisins and dates for 2 to 3 minutes; remove half for garnish.
Add the fine vermicelli to the ghee and roast for 2 to 4 minutes until golden and fragrant.
Pour in the milk gradually and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook for 10 to 15 minutes, stirring often, until the vermicelli is tender.
Add sugar, cardamom and the fried fruits and nuts and simmer for another 5 minutes.
Add condensed milk or cream only if included and heat gently without boiling hard.
Serve warm or chilled, garnished with the reserved nuts and dates.

title: Seviyan
description: A practical home-style Seviyan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat ghee in a heavy pan and roast the vermicelli over medium-low heat for 3 to 5 minutes until evenly golden.
In a separate saucepan, heat the milk with cardamom until steaming.
Pour the hot milk over the roasted vermicelli carefully and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook for 7 to 10 minutes, stirring, until the strands are tender but still distinct.
Add sugar and cook for another 3 to 5 minutes until dissolved.
Stir in nuts and raisins and simmer for 1 minute.
Remove from the heat while slightly loose because it thickens on standing.
Serve warm or chill for 2 hours.

title: Zarda
description: A practical home-style Zarda recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/4 tsp yellow food colour or saffron
steps: Rinse and soak basmati rice for 30 minutes. Boil in coloured or saffron water with cardamom until 90% cooked and drain.
Heat ghee in a wide pot and add cloves and cardamom for 30 seconds.
Add sugar with a small amount of water and heat gently until dissolved.
Fold in the drained rice without breaking the grains.
Add raisins, nuts, candied fruit and small pieces of khoya if included.
Cover tightly and steam on the lowest heat for 12 to 15 minutes until the syrup is absorbed.
Rest covered for 10 minutes and fluff carefully.
Serve warm with the garnishes spread evenly through the rice.

title: Shahi Tukray
description: A practical home-style Shahi Tukray recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Trim the bread crusts and cut each slice diagonally. Dry the pieces in a 150°C (300°F) oven for 10 minutes so they absorb less oil.
Shallow-fry in ghee over medium heat until crisp and evenly golden, then drain.
Boil sugar and water for 5 to 6 minutes with cardamom to make a light syrup; dip the fried bread for only 10 to 15 seconds per side.
Simmer milk in a wide pan for 30 to 45 minutes, scraping in the cream from the sides, until reduced by nearly half.
Add sugar, saffron and cardamom to the reduced milk and cook 5 minutes more.
Arrange the syrup-dipped bread in a serving dish and pour the warm rabri over it.
Garnish with almonds, pistachios and silver leaf if used.
Rest for 30 minutes and serve warm or chilled.

title: Double Ka Meetha
description: A practical home-style Double Ka Meetha recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Cut bread slices into triangles or squares and let them dry uncovered for 1 hour.
Fry the bread in ghee over medium heat until deep golden and crisp, then drain well.
Prepare a light syrup by boiling sugar, water and cardamom for 6 minutes.
Dip each fried piece briefly in warm syrup and arrange in a shallow dish.
Simmer milk with saffron until reduced by one-third, then add khoya or cream if included.
Pour the thickened milk evenly over the bread and scatter nuts and raisins.
Bake at 180°C (350°F) for 10 to 15 minutes or cook covered over very low heat until the milk is mostly absorbed.
Serve warm or chilled after the bread has softened but still retains shape.

title: Gajar Ka Halwa
description: A practical home-style Gajar Ka Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Peel and grate the carrots finely. Use a wide heavy pan so moisture can evaporate efficiently.
Add carrots and milk and cook over medium heat for 35 to 50 minutes, stirring often, until the milk is almost completely absorbed.
Add sugar and continue cooking; the mixture will loosen as the sugar dissolves.
Cook for 12 to 15 minutes until the released syrup evaporates.
Add ghee and fry the halwa over medium-low heat for 10 minutes until glossy and pulling from the pan.
Stir in khoya, cardamom and most of the nuts and cook for 5 minutes.
Taste and stop when the carrots are tender but still have slight texture.
Serve warm, garnished with remaining nuts.

title: Suji Halwa
description: A practical home-style Suji Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat water or milk with sugar and cardamom until the sugar dissolves; keep the syrup hot.
Melt ghee in a heavy pan and add semolina.
Roast over medium-low heat for 8 to 12 minutes, stirring constantly, until golden and nutty.
Lower the heat and carefully add the hot syrup in several portions while stirring; it will bubble strongly.
Cook over low heat for 4 to 6 minutes until the semolina absorbs the liquid and the halwa pulls from the pan.
Stir in raisins and nuts and cover for 5 minutes.
Fluff with a spoon and adjust with a little hot water if it has become too firm.
Serve warm while the halwa is soft and glossy, garnished with additional nuts if desired.

title: Badam Halwa
description: A practical home-style Badam Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the almonds in hot water, peel them and grind with a little milk into a fine but not watery paste.
Heat half the ghee in a heavy non-stick pan and add the almond paste.
Cook over low heat for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring continuously, until the raw aroma disappears.
Add sugar and saffron milk; the mixture will loosen as the sugar melts.
Continue cooking for 15 to 20 minutes, adding the remaining ghee a spoonful at a time.
Stir constantly until the halwa becomes glossy, leaves the sides and holds a soft mound.
Add cardamom and chopped nuts and cook for 1 minute.
Serve warm or press into a greased tray and cut when set.

title: Moong Dal Halwa
description: A practical home-style Moong Dal Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 cup split yellow mung dal, soaked and ground
steps: Soak the split moong dal for 4 hours, drain thoroughly and grind to a coarse paste with minimal water.
Heat ghee in a heavy wide pan and add the dal paste.
Cook over medium-low heat for 25 to 35 minutes, stirring and breaking up lumps, until deep golden and fragrant.
Heat milk or water with sugar and saffron separately until the sugar dissolves.
Carefully add the hot liquid to the roasted dal in portions while stirring.
Cook for another 12 to 18 minutes until the liquid is absorbed and ghee begins to separate.
Add cardamom and nuts and fry for 2 minutes.
Serve hot; the texture should be rich and granular, not pasty.

title: Anday Ka Halwa
description: A practical home-style Anday Ka Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk the eggs, milk, sugar and cardamom for 2 minutes until the sugar begins to dissolve.
Melt ghee in a heavy non-stick pan over low heat.
Pour in the egg mixture and cook over low to medium-low heat, stirring continuously with a flat spatula.
As curds form, keep mashing and folding for 15 to 20 minutes so they become very fine rather than scrambled-egg pieces.
Continue cooking until the moisture evaporates, the colour deepens and ghee begins to separate.
Add khoya and nuts and cook for another 5 to 7 minutes until cohesive and aromatic.
Stir in saffron or kewra if used and remove from the heat.
Serve warm, garnished with pistachios.

title: Besan Halwa
description: A practical home-style Besan Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat ghee in a heavy pan over medium-low heat and add gram flour.
Roast for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring constantly and scraping the bottom, until the colour deepens and the aroma becomes nutty.
Heat milk or water with sugar and cardamom separately until the sugar dissolves.
Lower the heat and add the hot liquid gradually while whisking to prevent lumps.
Cook for 6 to 10 minutes until the besan absorbs the liquid and the halwa becomes glossy.
Add nuts and continue stirring until the mixture pulls away from the pan.
Rest covered for 5 minutes.
Serve warm with additional nuts.

title: Habshi Halwa
description: A practical home-style Habshi Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tbsp cocoa powder or caramelised sugar for colour
steps: Combine milk and sugar in a heavy wide pan and bring to a gentle simmer.
Whisk wheat germ or sprouted-wheat paste and flour with a little cold milk until smooth, then stir into the hot milk.
Cook over medium-low heat for 35 to 50 minutes, stirring continuously as the mixture thickens and darkens.
Add ghee in small additions, allowing each to absorb before adding more.
Continue cooking until the halwa is deep brown, glossy and begins to leave the sides of the pan.
Stir in cardamom, mace and chopped nuts and cook for 3 minutes.
Press into a greased tray, smooth the top and decorate with nuts.
Cool completely before cutting into dense squares.

title: Ras Malai
description: A practical home-style Ras Malai recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bring the milk for the chenna to a boil, lower the heat and add lemon juice gradually until the curds separate from clear whey.
Strain through muslin, rinse the curds under cold water and hang for 30 minutes; the chenna should be moist but not wet.
Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until completely smooth, then shape into small flat discs without cracks.
Boil sugar and water in a wide covered pan. Add the discs, cover and cook at a strong simmer for 12 to 15 minutes until doubled.
Meanwhile, simmer the second quantity of milk with sugar, cardamom and saffron until reduced by about one-quarter.
Cool the cooked discs slightly and press very gently to remove excess syrup.
Place them in the warm reduced milk and simmer on very low heat for 2 minutes, then cool.
Chill for at least 4 hours and serve with pistachios.

title: Gulab Jamun
description: A practical home-style Gulab Jamun recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix milk powder or crumbled khoya with flour, baking soda and ghee. Add milk gradually to form a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Rest the dough for 10 minutes, then divide and roll into smooth crack-free balls without compressing heavily.
Prepare syrup by boiling sugar and water for 6 minutes; add cardamom and rose water and keep it warm, not boiling.
Heat frying oil or ghee to 145–150°C (293–302°F).
Fry the balls in small batches for 7 to 10 minutes, moving them constantly, until evenly deep brown and cooked through.
Drain for 30 seconds and place the hot jamuns in the warm syrup.
Soak for at least 2 hours, turning once so they expand evenly.
Serve warm or at room temperature with syrup.

title: Kala Jamun
description: A practical home-style Kala Jamun recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tbsp cocoa powder or caramelised milk solids for darker colour
steps: Combine khoya or milk powder, flour, a pinch of baking soda and ghee, adding milk gradually to make a soft dough.
Knead only until smooth and rest for 10 minutes.
Shape small crack-free balls; fill with a tiny amount of chopped nuts if desired.
Prepare a warm cardamom syrup and keep it ready.
Heat oil or ghee to about 140°C (285°F) and fry very slowly for 10 to 14 minutes, turning continuously, until the exterior becomes dark mahogany without burning.
Check one ball to ensure the centre is cooked, then drain and transfer to warm syrup.
Soak for at least 3 hours so the darker crust softens and absorbs syrup.
Serve at room temperature.

title: Cham Cham
description: A practical home-style Cham Cham recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/2 cup khoya or thick cream for filling
steps: Curdle boiling milk gradually with lemon juice, strain through muslin and rinse the chenna thoroughly.
Hang for 30 minutes, then knead the moist chenna for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and free of grains.
Shape into small oval cylinders with no cracks.
Bring sugar and water to a vigorous boil in a wide covered pot. Add the ovals, cover and cook for 15 minutes until puffed.
Turn off the heat and leave them in syrup for 20 minutes, then cool completely.
Remove and drain lightly. Split lengthwise without cutting through and fill with sweetened khoya or cream if included.
Coat the sides with coconut and garnish with pistachios.
Chill for 2 hours before serving.

title: Rasgulla
description: A practical home-style Rasgulla recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bring milk to a boil, lower the heat and add diluted lemon juice gradually until soft curds separate.
Strain through muslin, rinse well to remove acidity and hang for 25 to 30 minutes.
Knead the chenna with the heel of the hand for 8 to 10 minutes until glossy and completely smooth.
Divide into small portions and roll into crack-free balls; they will nearly double in size.
Bring sugar and water to a strong boil in a wide pot. Add the balls, cover tightly and boil for 12 to 15 minutes.
Keep the syrup boiling and add a small splash of hot water if it becomes too concentrated.
Turn off the heat and cool the rasgullas in the syrup without disturbing them.
Chill for at least 2 hours before serving.

title: Jalebi
description: A practical home-style Jalebi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix flour, a little gram flour, yogurt and water into a smooth, thick pouring batter. Cover and ferment at warm room temperature for 8 to 12 hours.
Stir in a pinch of baking soda just before frying and adjust with water so the batter flows in an unbroken ribbon.
Prepare a one-string syrup by boiling sugar and water to about 105–106°C (221–223°F); add saffron, cardamom and lemon juice and keep warm.
Transfer batter to a squeeze bottle or piping bag with a small opening.
Heat oil or ghee to 175°C (350°F) and pipe connected spirals directly into the oil.
Fry for 1 to 2 minutes per side until crisp and golden.
Drain briefly, then soak in warm syrup for 20 to 30 seconds and lift to a rack.
Serve the jalebi warm while the exterior is still crisp and the syrup remains fragrant inside.

title: Imarti
description: A practical home-style Imarti recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 cups urad dal, soaked and ground
steps: Soak urad dal for 4 to 5 hours, drain and grind with very little water into a thick, exceptionally smooth batter.
Beat the batter for 5 minutes until light and fluffy; add colour if used.
Prepare a sticky one-string sugar syrup with cardamom and saffron and keep it warm.
Transfer the batter to a cloth piping bag or squeeze bottle with a small opening.
Heat ghee or oil to 165–170°C (330–340°F).
Pipe a small central ring and overlapping loops around it to form the traditional flower shape.
Fry slowly on both sides until crisp and cooked through, then place directly in warm syrup for 45 to 60 seconds.
Drain on a rack and serve warm or at room temperature.

title: Rabri
description: A practical home-style Rabri recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Pour the milk into the widest heavy pan available and bring it to a boil over medium heat.
Reduce to a gentle simmer. As a skin forms, push it to the side of the pan rather than stirring it back immediately.
Continue simmering for 60 to 90 minutes, scraping the bottom every 2 to 3 minutes so the milk does not scorch and collecting successive cream layers on the sides.
When the milk has reduced to roughly one-third, add sugar, cardamom and saffron and stir until dissolved.
Scrape all the collected cream layers back into the reduced milk in broad flakes.
Cook for another 5 minutes; the rabri should be thick but still spoonable and will thicken more when cold.
Cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 3 hours.
Serve chilled with pistachios and almonds.

title: Firni
description: A practical home-style Firni recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Wash the rice, soak for 30 minutes and grind with 1/2 cup milk into a fine, slightly grainy paste.
Bring the remaining milk to a gentle boil in a heavy pot.
Lower the heat and whisk in the rice paste slowly so no lumps form.
Cook over low heat for 18 to 25 minutes, stirring continuously near the end, until the rice is fully cooked and the mixture coats a spoon.
Add sugar, cardamom and saffron and cook for another 5 minutes.
Stir in rose or kewra water after removing from direct heat.
Pour into dampened earthen bowls or serving dishes and cool for 30 minutes.
Refrigerate for at least 4 hours and garnish with nuts before serving.

title: Kulfi
description: A practical home-style Kulfi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bring the milk to a boil in a wide heavy pan, then reduce to a steady low simmer.
Cook for 45 to 60 minutes, stirring and scraping the sides frequently, until reduced by about half.
Add sugar, cardamom and saffron and stir until dissolved.
Add khoya or milk powder if included and whisk until completely smooth; simmer for 5 minutes.
Stir in chopped nuts and cool the mixture over an ice bath, stirring occasionally to prevent a skin.
Pour into kulfi moulds, cover and freeze for at least 8 hours.
Dip each mould in cool-to-lukewarm water for 5 to 8 seconds and loosen with a thin knife.
Serve immediately before the dense kulfi begins to soften.

title: Pista Kulfi
description: A practical home-style Pista Kulfi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the pistachios in hot water for 15 minutes, peel if desired and grind half into a coarse paste while chopping the rest.
Simmer the milk in a wide pan for 45 to 60 minutes, stirring and scraping, until reduced by half.
Add sugar, cardamom, saffron and the pistachio paste and cook for 5 to 7 minutes.
Whisk in khoya or milk powder until smooth and simmer for another 3 minutes.
Fold in the chopped pistachios and cool the base completely over an ice bath.
Fill moulds, cover and freeze for 8 hours or overnight.
Unmould after dipping briefly in lukewarm water.
Serve with extra chopped pistachios.

title: Mango Kulfi
description: A practical home-style Mango Kulfi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Reduce the milk in a wide heavy pan over low heat for 35 to 50 minutes until nearly half remains.
Add sugar and cardamom and simmer for 5 minutes, then remove from the heat.
Whisk in khoya or milk powder until smooth and cool the mixture completely; warm milk can dull or split fresh mango.
Blend ripe mango pulp until smooth and measure it accurately.
Fold the mango pulp into the cold milk base and taste before adding more sugar.
Pour into kulfi moulds, cover tightly and freeze for at least 8 hours.
Dip moulds briefly in lukewarm water and unmould onto chilled plates.
Serve immediately with pistachios if desired.

title: Falooda
description: A practical home-style Falooda recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak basil seeds in plenty of cool water for 15 to 20 minutes until fully swollen, then drain.
Cook the falooda vermicelli in boiling water until just tender, rinse under cold water and drain well.
Prepare the rose milk by stirring rose syrup into thoroughly chilled milk; taste before adding extra sugar.
Place a spoonful of basil seeds and vermicelli in the bottom of each tall glass.
Add jelly cubes if included and pour in the cold rose milk, leaving room at the top.
Add one or two scoops of kulfi or ice cream.
Drizzle with additional rose syrup and garnish with nuts.
Serve immediately with a long spoon and straw.

title: Kashmiri Chai with Cream
description: A practical home-style Kashmiri Chai with Cream recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Place green tea leaves, water, baking soda, cardamom and star anise if used in a deep pot and boil vigorously for 10 minutes.
Add cold water in small amounts while whisking or pouring the tea repeatedly from a height to aerate it.
Continue boiling and aerating for 20 to 30 minutes until the concentrate becomes deep burgundy.
Strain the concentrate, return it to the pot and add milk gradually while stirring; the colour should change to pink.
Add sugar for a sweet version or salt for the traditional savoury version, then simmer gently for 5 minutes.
Whisk in part of the cream over low heat without allowing a hard boil.
Pour into cups and top each with a small spoonful of remaining cream and chopped nuts.
Serve piping hot before the cream cools, stirring gently at the table if a uniform richness is preferred.

title: Lab-e-Shireen
description: A practical home-style Lab-e-Shireen recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Prepare the jelly according to its packet directions, chill until fully set and cut into small cubes.
Cook the coloured vermicelli until just tender, rinse under cold water and drain.
Whisk the chilled cream and condensed milk for 1 minute until smooth; do not whip to stiff peaks.
Fold in chilled custard or milk if included to create a pourable creamy base.
Add fruit cocktail or fresh fruit, jelly cubes and vermicelli and fold gently.
Chill for at least 2 hours so the flavours blend without the fruit becoming mushy.
Add banana only shortly before serving because it darkens and softens quickly.
Serve cold with nuts or jelly on top.

title: Custard Trifle
description: A practical home-style Custard Trifle recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bake or prepare the sponge cake, cool completely and cut into even cubes.
Whisk custard powder with a portion of cold milk until smooth. Heat the remaining milk with sugar until steaming.
Pour in the custard slurry while whisking and cook over medium-low heat for 3 to 5 minutes until thick; cool with film touching the surface.
Prepare the jelly, chill until firm and cut into cubes. Drain canned fruit thoroughly.
Arrange sponge in a deep glass dish and moisten lightly with fruit juice or syrup without soaking it to mush.
Layer fruit, jelly and cooled custard, repeating until the dish is filled.
Whip cold cream on medium-high speed for 2 to 3 minutes to soft or medium peaks and spread over the top.
Chill for at least 4 hours and garnish immediately before serving.

title: Fruit Custard
description: A practical home-style Fruit Custard recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk the custard powder with 1/2 cup cold milk until no lumps remain.
Heat the remaining milk and sugar in a heavy saucepan until steaming.
Pour in the custard mixture in a thin stream while whisking constantly.
Cook over medium-low heat for 3 to 5 minutes until the custard coats a spoon, then stir for 1 minute off the heat.
Cool completely with plastic film touching the surface, then refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Cut the fruit into even pieces and drain any canned fruit well.
Fold fruit into the cold custard shortly before serving; add bananas and apples last.
Serve chilled with nuts or jelly if desired.

title: Mutanjan
description: A practical home-style Mutanjan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/2 cup small gulab jamun or sweet boondi
steps: Rinse and soak basmati rice for 30 minutes. Boil it in coloured water until about 90% cooked and drain.
Heat ghee in a wide pot and fry cardamom and cloves for 30 seconds.
Add sugar with a little water and heat until dissolved into a clear syrup.
Fold in the rice, then add raisins, nuts, candied fruit, dried coconut and small sweet meatballs or gulab jamun if included.
Dot with different food colours in separate areas rather than mixing them uniformly.
Cover and steam over the lowest heat for 12 to 15 minutes until the syrup is absorbed.
Rest for 10 minutes, then fluff very gently to create a multicoloured appearance.
Serve warm with the fruit and sweets evenly distributed.

title: Sohan Halwa
description: A practical home-style Sohan Halwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Grease a heavy tray and keep nuts ready. Dissolve sugar in the water or milk and bring to a simmer.
Whisk cornflour or wheat-sprout flour with cold milk until smooth, then add it gradually to the hot liquid.
Cook over medium-low heat, stirring continuously, until the mixture thickens and becomes translucent.
Add ghee a little at a time over 30 to 45 minutes, stirring until each addition is absorbed.
Continue cooking until the halwa becomes deep amber, glossy, elastic and begins to leave the pan.
Add cardamom and most of the nuts and cook for 2 minutes.
Spread quickly in the greased tray, press to an even thickness and decorate with remaining nuts.
Cool completely before cutting into firm pieces.

title: Patisa
description: A practical home-style Patisa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Grease a tray and keep it ready. Sift gram flour and plain flour together.
Heat ghee over medium-low heat, add the flours and roast for 12 to 15 minutes until aromatic and pale golden; keep warm.
Boil sugar and water without stirring until the syrup reaches a firm two-string stage, about 120–122°C (248–252°F).
Pour the hot syrup into the roasted flour mixture and fold rapidly with a broad spatula.
Continue lifting, folding and pulling as the mixture cools and becomes flaky; avoid pressing it into a dense paste.
Transfer to the greased tray and spread lightly without compacting the layers.
Scatter pistachios over the top and let set for 2 to 3 hours.
Cut or break into delicate flaky pieces and store airtight.

title: Rewari
description: A practical home-style Rewari recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Toast the sesame seeds in a dry pan over low heat for 3 to 4 minutes until fragrant; do not brown them deeply.
Grease a tray, rolling pin and small spoon before cooking the syrup.
Combine sugar or jaggery with water and cook without stirring until it reaches hard-crack stage, 150°C (302°F).
Remove from the heat and stir in a small amount of ghee and cardamom, then immediately add the sesame seeds.
Work quickly because the mixture hardens fast. Drop tiny portions onto the greased tray or roll thin and cut small discs.
Allow to cool completely without stacking.
Separate any pieces that joined while still slightly warm.
Store airtight away from moisture to preserve the crisp snap.

title: Bakarkhani
description: A practical home-style Bakarkhani recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix flour, sugar, salt and yeast if included. Add warm milk and enough water to make a firm dough, then knead for 8 to 10 minutes.
Cover and rest for 45 to 60 minutes. Divide into portions and roll each into a thin oval.
Brush with ghee, fold into layers and rest for 10 minutes to relax the dough.
Roll each layered portion to about 5 mm thick and prick the surface with a fork.
Brush with milk or egg wash and sprinkle sesame or poppy seeds.
Bake on a preheated tray at 220°C (425°F) for 10 to 14 minutes until puffed and deep golden.
Brush lightly with ghee as soon as they leave the oven.
Cool on a rack so the outer layers remain crisp.

title: Sheermal
description: A practical home-style Sheermal recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bloom the yeast in warm milk with a pinch of sugar for 5 to 10 minutes.
Mix flour, sugar, salt, saffron milk and cardamom. Add the yeast mixture and ghee and knead for 10 minutes until smooth and soft.
Cover and rise in a warm place for 60 to 90 minutes until puffy.
Divide into balls, rest for 10 minutes and roll into rounds about 6 mm thick.
Prick all over with a fork and brush generously with milk.
Bake at 220°C (425°F) on a hot tray or stone for 8 to 12 minutes until golden with darker spots.
Brush with ghee immediately and cover with a clean cloth for 5 minutes.
Serve warm with korma or tea.

title: Taftan
description: A practical home-style Taftan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix yeast and sugar into warm milk and leave for 5 minutes until foamy.
Combine flour, salt, saffron or cardamom and ghee. Add the yeast milk and egg if included and knead for 10 minutes.
Cover and let rise for 60 to 90 minutes until doubled.
Divide into balls, rest for 10 minutes and flatten into thick rounds with a slightly raised rim.
Press decorative lines or holes into the surface and brush with egg wash or milk.
Sprinkle sesame or nigella seeds and bake at 220°C (425°F) for 10 to 14 minutes until richly golden.
Brush with ghee and cool briefly under a cloth.
Serve the taftan warm while its crust is tender and the saffron-enriched crumb remains soft.

title: Kulcha
description: A practical home-style Kulcha recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix flour, salt, sugar, baking powder and yogurt. Add water gradually and knead for 8 minutes into a soft dough.
Add oil and knead for another 2 minutes, then cover and rest for at least 1 hour.
Divide into balls and rest 10 minutes. Roll or pat each into a round or oval about 5 mm thick.
Brush one side lightly with water and sprinkle the other with coriander, sesame or nigella seeds.
Place the wet side against a hot tawa and cook until bubbles form.
Invert the tawa over a gas flame or place under a very hot broiler until the top develops brown blisters; alternatively bake at 250°C (480°F).
Remove when cooked through and brush with butter.
Serve immediately while soft and slightly chewy.

title: Roghni Naan
description: A practical home-style Roghni Naan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bloom yeast and sugar in warm milk for 5 to 10 minutes.
Mix flour, salt, yogurt, egg if included and ghee. Add the yeast milk and knead for 10 minutes until smooth.
Cover the enriched roghni naan dough and let it rise for 60–90 minutes, until doubled and visibly aerated.
Divide into balls, rest 10 minutes and press into thick oval naans.
Make deep fingertip dimples across the surface, brush with milk or egg wash and scatter sesame seeds.
Bake on a preheated stone or tray at 240°C (465°F) for 8 to 12 minutes until puffed and golden.
Brush immediately with ghee or butter.
Cover for 3 minutes before serving so the crumb stays tender.

title: Tandoori Naan
description: A practical home-style Tandoori Naan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Dissolve yeast and sugar in warm water and leave until foamy, about 5 to 10 minutes.
Mix flour and salt, then add yogurt, oil and yeast water. Knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Cover the naan dough and ferment it for 60–90 minutes, until doubled and soft enough to stretch without tearing.
Heat a pizza stone or heavy tray at the oven's highest setting, ideally 250°C (480°F), for at least 30 minutes.
Divide the dough, rest 10 minutes and stretch each piece into a teardrop, leaving the rim slightly thicker.
Brush one side with water and place wet-side down on the hot stone or tawa.
Bake or broil for 3 to 5 minutes until puffed with charred spots and fully cooked.
Brush with butter and serve immediately.

title: Garlic Naan
description: A practical home-style Garlic Naan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Prepare a soft yeasted naan dough with flour, yeast, sugar, salt, yogurt, oil and warm water; knead for 8 to 10 minutes.
Cover and let rise until doubled, about 60 to 90 minutes.
Mix minced garlic with chopped coriander and melted butter; reserve half the butter for after baking.
Divide the dough and rest the balls for 10 minutes.
Stretch each into an oval and press garlic and coriander firmly onto the top.
Bake on a very hot stone or tray at 250°C (480°F), or cook on a hot tawa and finish under the broiler, for 3 to 5 minutes.
Brush with the reserved garlic butter immediately.
Serve hot before the edges harden.

title: Cheese Naan
description: A practical home-style Cheese Naan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Knead flour, yeast, sugar, salt, yogurt, oil and warm water for 8 to 10 minutes into a smooth soft dough.
Cover and rise for 60 to 90 minutes, then divide into equal balls and rest 10 minutes.
Flatten one ball, place grated mozzarella or mild cheese in the centre and pinch the edges tightly over the filling.
Rest seam-side down for 5 minutes, then roll gently into an oval without tearing or exposing cheese.
Brush one side with water and cook on a very hot tawa or preheated stone.
Finish under a high broiler or in a 250°C (480°F) oven until puffed and browned, about 4 to 6 minutes.
Brush with butter and rest for 1 minute so the filling settles.
Serve hot while the cheese is melted.

title: Plain Naan
description: A practical home-style Plain Naan recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix flour, yeast, sugar and salt. Add yogurt, oil and warm water and knead for 8 to 10 minutes until elastic.
Cover and let rise in a warm place for 60 to 90 minutes.
Heat a baking stone or heavy tray at 250°C (480°F) for at least 30 minutes.
Divide the dough into balls, cover and rest for 10 minutes.
Stretch each ball into a teardrop about 5 mm thick, keeping some air in the rim.
Bake directly on the hot surface for 3 to 5 minutes until puffed and browned in spots.
Brush with butter or ghee as soon as it comes out.
Stack under a clean cloth and serve warm.

title: Khameeri Roti
description: A practical home-style Khameeri Roti recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix flour, yeast, sugar and salt. Add warm water gradually and knead for 10 minutes into a soft elastic dough.
Cover and ferment for 1 1/2 to 2 hours until doubled and slightly tangy.
Punch down gently, divide into balls and rest for 15 minutes.
Pat each ball into a thick round, using flour sparingly so the surface remains moist.
Prick the centre or make shallow finger indentations to control puffing.
Bake on a hot stone at 240°C (465°F) for 6 to 9 minutes or cook in a tandoor until puffed and browned.
Brush lightly with water or ghee and cover for 5 minutes.
Serve the khameeri roti warm with curries while its fermented crumb is still soft and lightly chewy.

title: Rusks
description: A practical home-style Rusks recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bake or use a firm plain loaf and cool it completely, preferably overnight, so it slices cleanly.
Heat the oven to 140°C (285°F) and line baking trays.
Cut the loaf into even 1 to 1.5 cm slices with a serrated knife.
Arrange in a single layer without overlap.
Bake for 20 minutes, turn every slice and bake another 20 to 30 minutes.
Reduce the temperature to 120°C (250°F) if the edges brown before the centres dry.
Turn off the oven and leave the rusks inside for 10 minutes with the door slightly open.
Cool completely before storing airtight; they should be dry and crisp throughout.

title: Cake Rusk
description: A practical home-style Cake Rusk recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease and line a loaf pan.
Beat softened butter and sugar on medium-high speed for 3 to 4 minutes until pale and fluffy.
Beat in the eggs one at a time for about 30 seconds each, then add vanilla or cardamom.
Whisk flour, baking powder and salt and fold into the batter alternately with milk, mixing only until smooth.
Bake for 30 to 40 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool completely, then chill or rest overnight.
Slice into 1.5 cm pieces and arrange on trays. Reduce oven to 140°C (285°F).
Bake 20 minutes per side, then dry at 120°C (250°F) for 10 to 20 minutes if needed.
Cool the cake rusks completely before storing them airtight so residual steam cannot soften their twice-baked crumb.

title: Nan Khatai
description: A practical home-style Nan Khatai recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oven to 165°C (330°F) and line two trays.
Beat ghee and powdered sugar for 3 to 4 minutes until pale and creamy.
Whisk flour, gram flour, semolina, baking powder, cardamom and a pinch of salt.
Mix the dry ingredients into the creamed ghee just until a soft, non-sticky dough forms; do not add excess liquid.
Shape small balls, flatten slightly and press a pistachio or almond into each centre.
Chill for 15 minutes so they hold their shape.
Bake for 16 to 20 minutes until the tops crack and the bases are lightly golden while the tops remain pale.
Cool on the tray for 10 minutes before moving.

title: Butter Biscuits
description: A practical home-style Butter Biscuits recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oven to 175°C (350°F) and line baking trays.
Beat softened butter and sugar on medium speed for 3 minutes until light and smooth.
Beat in the egg and vanilla for 30 seconds, scraping the bowl.
Whisk flour, baking powder and salt and mix into the butter mixture on low speed only until a dough forms.
Chill for 20 to 30 minutes, then roll to 5 mm and cut shapes or pipe with a large star nozzle.
Arrange 3 cm apart and bake for 10 to 14 minutes until the edges are pale golden.
Cool 5 minutes on the tray, then transfer to a rack.
Store only when completely cool.

title: Coconut Biscuits
description: A practical home-style Coconut Biscuits recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oven to 170°C (340°F) and line two trays.
Beat butter and sugar for 3 minutes until pale, then beat in the egg and vanilla.
Whisk flour, baking powder, salt and desiccated coconut.
Mix the dry ingredients into the butter mixture until evenly combined; add a spoonful of milk only if too crumbly to shape.
Roll into small balls, flatten slightly and coat the tops with extra coconut.
Chill for 15 minutes to limit spreading.
Bake for 12 to 16 minutes until lightly golden around the edges and aromatic.
Cool completely on a rack.

title: Karachi Fruit Cake
description: A practical home-style Karachi Fruit Cake recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oven to 165°C (330°F). Grease and double-line a loaf or round cake pan.
Toss the chopped dried fruit and nuts with 1 tablespoon of the measured flour so they remain suspended.
Beat softened butter and sugar for 4 to 5 minutes until very light and fluffy.
Beat in eggs one at a time, mixing for 30 seconds after each and scraping the bowl; add vanilla and citrus zest.
Whisk the remaining flour with baking powder and spices and fold into the batter alternately with milk or juice.
Fold in the coated fruit and nuts and spread evenly in the pan.
Bake for 60 to 80 minutes until a skewer comes out without wet batter and the centre reaches about 96°C (205°F).
Cool in the pan for 15 minutes, then unmould and cool completely before slicing.

title: Plain Sponge Cake
description: A practical home-style Plain Sponge Cake recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease the base of a 20 cm pan, line with parchment and leave the sides mostly ungreased for better rise.
Bring eggs to room temperature. Beat eggs and sugar on high speed for 6 to 8 minutes until very pale, tripled and able to hold a ribbon for several seconds.
Sift flour, baking powder and salt twice.
Fold the dry ingredients into the egg foam in three additions with a large spatula, turning the bowl and preserving as much air as possible.
Fold a small portion of batter into melted butter or oil, then gently fold that mixture back into the main batter with vanilla.
Pour immediately into the pan and bake for 25 to 32 minutes until golden, springy and a skewer comes out clean.
Cool 10 minutes, loosen the edge and turn onto a rack.
Cool completely before filling or slicing.

title: Cream Rolls
description: A practical home-style Cream Rolls recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat the oven to 200°C (400°F) and lightly grease metal cream-horn moulds.
Roll cold puff pastry to about 3 mm thick and cut it into strips about 2 cm wide.
Wrap each strip around a mould with a slight overlap, starting at the pointed end and avoiding gaps.
Brush with beaten egg and sprinkle lightly with sugar.
Bake for 15 to 18 minutes until puffed and deep golden. Cool for 5 minutes, remove moulds and cool shells completely.
Whip very cold cream, powdered sugar and vanilla on medium-high speed for 2 to 3 minutes until stiff peaks form.
Pipe cream into both ends only shortly before serving.
Keep filled rolls chilled and store unfilled shells airtight.

title: Patties
description: A practical home-style Patties recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 cups cooked mixed vegetables, finely chopped
steps: Prepare the vegetable filling by cooking onion in oil for 4 minutes, adding cumin and vegetables and frying until completely dry.
Season with chilli, garam masala and salt and cool the filling fully; warm filling melts puff-pastry layers.
Heat the oven to 200°C (400°F) and line a tray.
Roll cold puff pastry to about 3 mm and cut into equal rectangles.
Place filling on one half, brush edges with beaten egg, fold over and press firmly with a fork.
Arrange on the tray, brush the tops with egg and cut a small steam vent.
Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until well puffed and golden, rotating once if needed.
Cool for 5 minutes before serving.

title: Chicken Patties
description: A practical home-style Chicken Patties recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Cook onion in butter or oil for 4 minutes, add ginger or garlic if included and stir for 30 seconds.
Add cooked shredded chicken, black pepper, salt and flour and stir for 1 minute.
Pour in milk gradually and cook until the filling is creamy but very thick and no liquid pools; cool completely.
Heat the oven to 200°C (400°F). Roll cold puff pastry to 3 mm and cut matching shapes.
Place a spoonful of filling in the centre, brush the edges with egg and cover with a second pastry piece.
Seal firmly, brush with egg and cut a small vent without cutting through the base.
Bake for 18 to 24 minutes until high, crisp and deep golden.
Rest 5 minutes before serving because the filling is very hot.

title: Lassi
description: A practical home-style Lassi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Place chilled yogurt, cold water or milk, sugar or salt according to the chosen version and ice in a blender.
Blend on high for 45 to 60 seconds until completely smooth and frothy.
Taste and adjust the thickness with a little more water or yogurt.
Add cardamom, roasted cumin or rose water only according to the intended flavour.
Blend for another 10 seconds to distribute the seasoning.
Pour immediately into chilled glasses from a slight height to keep the foam.
Top with a spoonful of cream or crushed ice if desired.
Serve at once; do not let the ice dilute it.

title: Sweet Lassi
description: A practical home-style Sweet Lassi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Use cold, thick plain yogurt and whisk it briefly to remove lumps.
Add yogurt, chilled milk or water, sugar, cardamom and ice to a blender.
Blend on high for 45 seconds until the sugar dissolves and a thick foam forms.
Taste and add sugar gradually rather than making it cloying.
Add rose water only a few drops at a time and blend for 5 seconds.
Adjust with milk for a richer drink or water for a lighter one.
Pour into chilled glasses and garnish with cardamom or cream.
Serve immediately while the lassi is cold, thick, and capped with its freshly blended foam.

title: Salted Lassi
description: A practical home-style Salted Lassi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk cold yogurt until smooth before adding it to the blender.
Add chilled water, salt, roasted cumin, black pepper, mint and ice.
Blend for 40 to 50 seconds until frothy and evenly seasoned.
Taste for salt and cumin; add more water if the drink is too thick.
Crush a few mint leaves between the fingers and stir them in for a fresher aroma.
Pour into chilled glasses from a height to preserve foam.
Sprinkle a small pinch of roasted cumin on top.
Serve immediately with savoury food.

title: Mango Lassi
description: A practical home-style Mango Lassi recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Peel and cut ripe mango, then blend the pulp alone until completely smooth.
Add cold yogurt, chilled milk, sugar only if needed, cardamom and ice.
Blend on high for 45 to 60 seconds until thick, smooth and frothy.
Taste; sour yogurt may need more mango or sugar, while very sweet mango may need none.
Adjust the consistency with milk one tablespoon at a time.
Chill for 10 minutes only if all ingredients were not already cold.
Pour into chilled glasses and garnish with pistachios or mango cubes.
Serve immediately while the mango lassi is cold, creamy, and evenly suspended without separation.

title: Rooh Afza Milk
description: A practical home-style Rooh Afza Milk recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Chill the milk thoroughly before mixing so no extra ice is required.
Pour the milk into a jug and add Rooh Afza syrup gradually.
Whisk for 30 seconds or blend for 10 to 15 seconds until the colour is even and a light foam forms.
Taste before adding sugar because the syrup is already sweet.
Add soaked basil seeds if included and stir rather than blend them.
Add ice only just before serving to avoid dilution.
Pour into chilled glasses and garnish with nuts or rose petals if desired.
Serve immediately while the milk is thoroughly chilled and the floral syrup remains evenly mixed.

title: Rooh Afza Sharbat
description: A practical home-style Rooh Afza Sharbat recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Fill a jug with thoroughly chilled water and add Rooh Afza syrup a little at a time.
Stir for 30 to 45 seconds until completely dissolved.
Add fresh lemon juice if desired to balance the sweetness.
Taste and adjust with more water or syrup; the flavour should be fragrant but not heavy.
Add soaked basil seeds only after they have fully swollen and been drained.
Add ice immediately before serving.
Stir once more and pour into chilled glasses.
Serve at once while the sharbat is cold and fragrant, before melting ice weakens the flavour.

title: Doodh Soda
description: A practical home-style Doodh Soda recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Chill the milk, clear lemon-lime soda and glasses until very cold; warm ingredients can curdle or foam excessively.
Pour the cold milk into a tall jug, filling it no more than halfway.
Add sugar or rose syrup and stir until dissolved before adding soda.
Tilt the jug and pour in the cold soda slowly to control the foam.
Stir only once or twice with a long spoon so most carbonation remains.
Add ice sparingly because it dilutes the drink.
Pour immediately into chilled glasses, leaving room for foam.
Serve at once; do not store after mixing.

title: Lemon Mint Drink
description: A practical home-style Lemon Mint Drink recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Make a light syrup by dissolving sugar in a small amount of warm water, then cool it completely.
Blend fresh mint, lemon juice, cooled syrup, black salt and half the cold water for 30 seconds.
Strain for a smooth drink or leave unstrained for a stronger mint flavour.
Add the remaining cold water and taste for acidity, sweetness and salt.
Blend briefly with ice until the drink is slushy but not watery.
Pour into chilled glasses.
Garnish with mint and lemon slices.
Serve immediately before the mint darkens.

title: Sattu Drink
description: A practical home-style Sattu Drink recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Place roasted gram flour in a jug and whisk it first with 1/2 cup cool water to make a smooth paste.
Gradually whisk in the remaining chilled water so no dry lumps remain.
For the savoury version, add black salt, roasted cumin, lemon and mint; for the sweet version, dissolve sugar or jaggery first.
Whisk vigorously for 1 minute because sattu settles quickly.
Taste and adjust the drink to a light, pourable consistency.
Add ice just before serving.
Stir each glass as it is poured to keep the sattu suspended.
Serve immediately, stirring once more before each pour because the roasted gram naturally settles.

title: Kashmiri Chai
description: A practical home-style Kashmiri Chai recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Boil green tea leaves, water, baking soda, cardamom and star anise if used for 10 minutes in a deep pot.
Add a cup of cold water and whisk vigorously, or ladle the tea from a height repeatedly to aerate it.
Continue boiling and aerating for 20 to 30 minutes until the concentrate is dark burgundy and reduced.
Strain the concentrate and return it to the pot.
Add milk gradually while stirring; the alkaline tea should turn a clear pink.
Season with sugar or salt and simmer gently for 5 minutes.
Pour into cups and garnish with almonds and pistachios.
Serve very hot while the pink colour is vivid and the cardamom and tea aromas are at their strongest.

title: Doodh Patti
description: A practical home-style Doodh Patti recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Combine milk and water in a saucepan and bring to a gentle boil.
Add black tea leaves or tea bags and reduce the heat.
Simmer for 3 to 5 minutes, watching closely so the milk does not boil over.
Add sugar and cardamom and simmer for another 1 to 2 minutes until the colour is deep and the tea tastes strong.
For extra body, lift and pour the tea with a ladle a few times, keeping it safely inside the pot.
Strain immediately so the leaves do not make it bitter.
Pour the strained doodh patti into warmed cups so it retains its heat and creamy body.
Serve hot immediately, before a skin forms on the milk-rich surface.

title: Green Tea—Kehwa
description: A practical home-style Green Tea—Kehwa recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bring water, cinnamon, cardamom and saffron to a gentle boil and simmer for 4 to 5 minutes.
Turn off the heat and let the water settle for 20 seconds.
Add green tea leaves and steep for 2 to 3 minutes; boiling the leaves hard makes kehwa bitter.
Strain into a clean pot or directly into cups.
Sweeten with honey or sugar only after straining.
Add slivered almonds to each cup.
Serve immediately while fragrant and clear.
Do not reboil prepared kehwa.

title: Almond Milkshake
description: A practical home-style Almond Milkshake recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak almonds in hot water for 20 minutes, peel and cool them.
Blend almonds with 1/2 cup cold milk for 45 to 60 seconds until very smooth.
Add the remaining chilled milk, sugar or honey, cardamom and ice cream if included.
Blend on high for another 30 to 45 seconds until thick and frothy.
Taste and adjust sweetness and consistency with more milk.
Strain only if a completely smooth texture is preferred.
Pour into chilled glasses and garnish with sliced almonds.
Serve immediately while the shake is cold, frothy, and the almond solids remain evenly suspended.

title: Sugarcane Juice
description: A practical home-style Sugarcane Juice recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Scrub and peel the outer surface of fresh sugarcane and rinse it thoroughly with potable water.
Cut into lengths suitable for the juicer and inspect for mould or fermentation; discard damaged pieces.
Pass the cane through a properly cleaned sugarcane press, following the equipment's safety instructions.
Collect the juice in a clean chilled container and strain through a fine sieve.
Stir in lemon juice, ginger or black salt only after pressing.
Add ice made from safe water and mix once.
Serve immediately because fresh sugarcane juice darkens and ferments quickly.
Discard juice that has stood warm for an extended period.

title: Falooda Drink
description: A practical home-style Falooda Drink recipe from Pakistani cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak basil seeds for 15 to 20 minutes until fully expanded, then drain.
Boil falooda vermicelli until tender, rinse under cold water and drain completely.
Mix chilled milk with rose syrup and taste before adding extra sugar.
Place vermicelli and basil seeds in tall glasses and add jelly cubes if included.
Pour in the rose milk and stir gently from the bottom.
Add crushed ice or a scoop of ice cream according to the intended drink consistency.
Garnish with nuts and an extra drizzle of rose syrup.
Serve immediately with a straw and long spoon.

title: Masala Dosa
description: A practical home-style Masala Dosa recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the dosa rice, urad dal, and fenugreek seeds in separate bowls for 4–6 hours. Drain, then grind the urad dal with cold water until very light and fluffy; grind the rice to a fine, slightly grainy batter.

Combine both batters in a deep bowl and beat by hand for 2 minutes to incorporate air. The batter should fall in a thick ribbon; cover loosely and ferment in a warm place for 8–12 hours, until risen and pleasantly sour.
For the potato masala, heat 2 tablespoons oil over medium heat. Crackle the mustard seeds, add curry leaves and green chillies, then sauté the sliced onion for 5–6 minutes until soft but not browned.
Add turmeric and the crushed boiled potatoes with 3–4 tablespoons water. Season with salt, fold gently, and cook for 4 minutes until moist but not wet; keep warm.
Stir salt into the fermented batter and thin it with a little water to a pourable consistency similar to light cream. Do not beat out all the fermentation bubbles.

Heat a cast-iron tawa over medium-high heat until a drop of water dances and evaporates. Wipe lightly with oil, pour about 1/3 cup batter in the centre, and spread rapidly in widening circles to a thin 20–23 cm dosa.
Drizzle 1 teaspoon oil around the edge and over the surface. Cook 2–3 minutes until the underside is deep golden and the edges release; place potato masala across the centre.
Fold the dosa over the filling and serve immediately while crisp, with coconut chutney and sambar.

title: Plain Dosa
description: A practical home-style Plain Dosa recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Wash the rice, urad dal, and fenugreek seeds well, then soak the rice and dal separately for 4–6 hours.
Grind the urad dal first with small additions of cold water until smooth, voluminous, and fluffy. Grind the rice to a smooth batter with a faint granular texture.
Mix the two batters thoroughly with clean hands for 2 minutes. Cover the bowl with room for expansion and ferment at 27–32°C for 8–12 hours, until doubled and aromatic.
Add salt and enough water to make a free-flowing batter that coats a spoon but is not watery. Rest it for 15 minutes after adjusting the consistency.
Heat a seasoned cast-iron tawa over medium-high heat. Sprinkle a few drops of water; when they evaporate instantly, wipe the surface with a cut onion dipped lightly in oil.
Pour 1/3 cup batter in the centre and spread it quickly into a thin circle using the base of the ladle. Drizzle a little oil around the edge.
Cook for 2–3 minutes until the base is crisp and golden. For a softer dosa, flip and cook the second side for 20 seconds; for a crisp dosa, leave it unflipped.
Roll or fold and serve at once, reheating and wiping the tawa between dosas so the batter spreads evenly.

title: Mysore Masala Dosa
description: A practical home-style Mysore Masala Dosa recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the rice, urad dal, and fenugreek for 4–6 hours, grind separately, combine, and ferment the batter in a warm place for 8–12 hours until well risen.
Prepare the potato filling by tempering mustard seeds, curry leaves, and green chillies in 2 tablespoons oil. Add onion and cook until translucent, then add turmeric and crushed potatoes.
Season the filling, add a splash of water, and cook 4–5 minutes until cohesive. Keep it soft enough to spread without tearing the dosa.
Stir salt into the fermented batter and add water little by little until it pours smoothly in a broad ribbon.
Heat a cast-iron tawa over medium-high heat, wipe it lightly with oil, and spread 1/3 cup batter into a thin 20–23 cm circle.
When the top loses its wet shine, drizzle oil around the edge and cook until the underside starts turning crisp and golden.
Spread 1–2 tablespoons red garlic chutney thinly over the dosa, leaving a 2 cm border. Add a line of potato masala through the centre and cook for another minute.
Fold into thirds or roll tightly and serve immediately; the defining result is a crisp dosa with a spicy chutney layer and soft potato centre.

title: Rava Dosa
description: A practical home-style Rava Dosa recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk semolina, rice flour, all-purpose flour, salt, cumin, black pepper, green chilli, and coriander in a large bowl.
Pour in the water gradually while whisking to remove flour pockets. The batter must be very thin and watery; rest it for 20–30 minutes so the semolina hydrates.
Whisk again and add more water if the batter has thickened. Stir before every dosa because the flours settle rapidly.
Heat a flat cast-iron tawa over medium-high heat until thoroughly hot, then rub it with a very thin film of oil.
Starting from the outer edge, pour the batter from 10–15 cm above the pan in a loose circular motion, allowing it to form a lacy network; do not spread it with the ladle.
Drizzle oil into the holes and around the edge. Cook undisturbed for 3–4 minutes until the surface dries and the base becomes crisp and deeply speckled.
Loosen carefully with a thin spatula. Fold without flipping, or flip for only 20–30 seconds if a firmer top is preferred.
Serve immediately; keep the batter thin and the pan hot for every batch to preserve the characteristic lace-like texture.

title: Neer Dosa
description: A practical home-style Neer Dosa recipe from Indian cuisine
