licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the raw rice and soak it in plenty of water for 4–6 hours, then drain completely.
Blend the soaked rice with grated coconut, if using, and about 1 cup of the measured water until completely smooth, rubbing a drop between the fingers to check for grit.
Transfer to a bowl and whisk in the remaining water and salt. The batter should be as thin as milk and does not require fermentation or resting.
Heat a well-seasoned tawa over medium-high heat and grease it very lightly. Stir the batter from the bottom before each dosa.
Pour a ladleful onto one side of the pan and immediately tilt and rotate the pan so the batter runs into a thin, irregular sheet; patch any large gaps with a few drops of batter.
Cover and steam-cook for 45–60 seconds, until the surface is no longer wet and the edges lift naturally. Do not brown heavily.
Fold the delicate dosa into quarters directly on the pan and lift it onto a plate. It is traditionally cooked on one side only.
Cool the pan for a few seconds between batches if the batter stops flowing, and stack the dosas loosely so they remain soft.

title: Set Dosa
description: A practical home-style Set Dosa recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the dosa rice, urad dal, and fenugreek seeds for 5 hours. Soak the flattened rice separately for the final 20 minutes.
Grind the urad dal until fluffy, then grind the rice and softened flattened rice to a smooth, thick batter. Mix both batters thoroughly.
Cover and ferment at warm room temperature for 8–12 hours, until the batter rises and shows fine bubbles throughout.
Add salt and enough water to reach a thick, pourable consistency similar to pancake batter; do not thin it as much as ordinary dosa batter.
Heat a tawa over medium heat and grease lightly. Pour a small ladleful without spreading it widely, forming a thick 10–12 cm round.
Drizzle a few drops of oil around the edge, cover, and cook for 1 1/2–2 minutes until the top is full of holes and nearly set.
Flip and cook the second side for 30–45 seconds, keeping the centre soft and spongy rather than crisp.
Serve in sets of two or three with vegetable saagu, coconut chutney, or sambar.

title: Idli
description: A practical home-style Idli recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Wash and soak the idli rice and fenugreek seeds together for 5–6 hours; soak the urad dal separately for the same time.
Grind the urad dal with ice-cold water in small additions until it is glossy, fluffy, and at least doubled in volume. Grind the rice to a thick batter with a fine semolina-like feel.
Mix the batters and salt vigorously by hand for 2–3 minutes. The combined batter should be thick enough to mound briefly before settling.
Cover loosely and ferment at 27–32°C for 8–12 hours, until increased in volume by about half and filled with tiny bubbles.
Gently fold the fermented batter once; do not beat it. Grease the idli moulds lightly and fill each cavity three-quarters full.
Bring 2–3 cm water to a strong simmer in the steamer before inserting the stand. Steam covered over medium heat for 10–12 minutes without opening the lid.
Turn off the heat and wait 2 minutes, then test with a skewer; it should emerge clean and the tops should spring back lightly.
Cool the moulds for 3–4 minutes before loosening with a wet spoon. Serve hot with sambar and chutney.

title: Medu Vada
description: A practical home-style Medu Vada recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the urad dal in cool water for 4 hours, then drain extremely well so excess water does not weaken the batter.
Grind the dal with only 2–4 tablespoons ice-cold water, adding it gradually, until the batter is smooth, thick, and very fluffy. Beat it by hand for 3–4 minutes to aerate.
Test a small spoonful in water; properly aerated batter should float. Fold in green chilli, ginger, pepper, curry leaves, onion, and salt just before frying.
Heat 5–7 cm oil in a deep pan to 175°C. Keep a bowl of water beside the stove and wet one hand lightly.
Place a lemon-sized portion of batter on the wet palm or a greased sheet, flatten gently, and make a clean hole through the centre with a wet finger.
Slide the shaped vada carefully into the oil. Fry only a few at a time, turning repeatedly for 4–5 minutes until evenly deep golden and crisp.
Maintain the oil between 170–180°C; cooler oil makes greasy vadas, while excessively hot oil leaves the centre undercooked.
Drain on a rack or absorbent paper and serve immediately, or soak briefly in hot water if preparing them for dahi vada.

title: Uttapam
description: A practical home-style Uttapam recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Use well-fermented dosa batter that has a mild sour aroma. Stir in salt and enough water to make it slightly thicker than ordinary dosa batter.
Combine the chopped onion, tomato, green chilli, and coriander in a bowl and season with a small pinch of salt just before cooking.
Heat a tawa over medium heat and grease it lightly. A lower temperature than for crisp dosa allows the thick centre to cook through.
Pour about 1/2 cup batter and spread only slightly to form a 15 cm round roughly 5–6 mm thick.
Immediately scatter a generous layer of the vegetable mixture over the wet surface and press it lightly with the back of the spatula.
Drizzle oil around the edge, cover, and cook for 2–3 minutes until the underside is golden and the top is mostly set.
Flip carefully and cook the vegetable side for 1–2 minutes until the onion softens and develops light browning without burning.
Serve hot with the vegetable side facing up, accompanied by coconut chutney and sambar.

title: Pongal
description: A practical home-style Pongal recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Dry-roast the split mung dal over medium-low heat for 3–4 minutes until nutty but not browned, then rinse it together with the rice.
Combine rice, dal, water, and salt in a heavy pot or pressure cooker. Pressure-cook for 4 whistles, or simmer covered for 35–40 minutes, until both grains collapse completely.
Open when pressure releases and mash vigorously with a ladle. Add hot water as needed to obtain a loose, porridge-like consistency; pongal thickens as it rests.
Heat ghee in a small pan over medium heat and fry the cashews until golden; remove and reserve them.
In the same ghee, add cumin, coarsely crushed black pepper, ginger, and curry leaves. Fry for 30–45 seconds until fragrant.
Pour the hot tempering into the rice-dal mixture and stir thoroughly so the pepper and ghee are evenly distributed.
Simmer for 2–3 minutes on low heat, adjusting salt and consistency. It should be soft, creamy, and spoonable rather than dry.
Garnish with the fried cashews and serve immediately with coconut chutney or sambar.

title: Upma
description: A practical home-style Upma recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Dry-roast the semolina over medium-low heat for 6–8 minutes, stirring continuously, until aromatic and sandy but still pale. Transfer to a plate.
Heat oil in the same pan over medium heat. Add mustard seeds; when they pop, add urad dal and fry until light golden.
Add curry leaves, green chilli, and onion. Sauté for 4–5 minutes until the onion is soft and translucent.
Pour in the measured water and salt, raise the heat, and bring to a full rolling boil.
Reduce the heat to low. Sprinkle the roasted semolina into the boiling water in a steady stream while whisking continuously to prevent lumps.
Cover and cook on low heat for 3–4 minutes, then uncover and fold gently until the grains are swollen and no dry patches remain.
Turn off the heat, cover, and rest for 5 minutes so the steam finishes cooking the semolina.
Fluff with a fork, mix in coriander, and serve hot; a spoonful of ghee or lemon juice may be added at the table.

title: Appam
description: A practical home-style Appam recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse and soak the raw rice for 5–6 hours, then drain. Blend it with the cooked rice, coconut milk, and enough water to make a very smooth batter.
Dissolve the yeast and sugar in 2 tablespoons lukewarm water at 38–40°C; leave for 5–10 minutes until foamy, then stir it into the batter.
Cover and ferment in a warm place for 6–8 hours, until the batter has risen and smells lightly fermented rather than sharply sour.
Add salt and enough water to make a batter thinner than pancake batter but thicker than milk. Rest for 15 minutes.
Heat an appam pan over medium heat and wipe it with a trace of oil. Pour about 1/3 cup batter into the centre.
Lift the pan by its handles and rotate it once so a thin layer climbs the sides while a thicker pool remains in the centre.
Cover and cook for 2–3 minutes without flipping, until the lacy rim is crisp and the soft centre is fully set.
Lift out gently and serve at once with vegetable stew, kadala curry, or coconut milk.

title: Puttu
description: A practical home-style Puttu recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix salt into the rice flour. Sprinkle warm water over it a tablespoon at a time while rubbing between the fingertips so the flour forms damp, loose crumbs rather than a dough.
Check the moisture by squeezing a handful: it should hold together briefly, then crumble when touched. Rest the moistened flour for 10 minutes.
Break up any large lumps and pass the mixture through a coarse sieve or pulse it once in a food processor for an even granular texture.
Bring water to a steady boil in the lower chamber of a puttu steamer.
Place the perforated disc in the cylinder. Add 2 tablespoons coconut, then a 4–5 cm layer of moistened rice flour; repeat the layers, ending with coconut.
Attach the cylinder to the steaming base, cover, and steam over medium-high heat for 5–7 minutes after steam begins escaping from the top.
Remove the cylinder, rest for 1 minute, and push the puttu out gently with the provided rod so the layers remain intact.
Serve hot with kadala curry, ripe banana, or sugar and coconut milk.

title: Avial
description: A practical home-style Avial recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 5 cups mixed vegetables cut into batons
steps: Cut the vegetables into even 5 cm batons, keeping quick-cooking and firm vegetables separate. This uniform shape is essential to the traditional texture.
Grind coconut, cumin, and green chillies with 3–4 tablespoons water to a coarse paste; it should retain visible coconut texture.
Place the firm vegetables in a wide pot with turmeric, salt, and about 1/2 cup water. Cover and cook over medium-low heat for 8–10 minutes.
Add the tender vegetables and continue cooking for 6–8 minutes, turning gently, until all are just tender and little free water remains.
Fold in the coconut paste and simmer uncovered for 3–4 minutes; avoid vigorous stirring that would break the vegetables.
Beat the yogurt briefly until smooth. Lower the heat completely and fold it into the vegetables, warming for 1–2 minutes without allowing it to boil.
Turn off the heat, scatter curry leaves, and drizzle coconut oil over the surface. Cover for 10 minutes so the aroma infuses.
Serve warm or at room temperature; avial should be thick, lightly tangy, and vegetable-forward rather than saucy.

title: Sambar
description: A practical home-style Sambar recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the toor dal and cook it with turmeric and 3 cups water until completely soft, about 25–30 minutes in a pot or 3–4 pressure-cooker whistles. Mash smooth.
Cook the mixed vegetables and tomato in a separate pot with 1 1/2 cups water and salt, adding firm vegetables first, until just tender.
Dilute the tamarind paste in 1/2 cup warm water and strain if fibrous. Add it to the vegetables and simmer for 5 minutes to cook away the raw sourness.
Add sambar powder and the mashed dal. Stir in enough hot water to make a medium-bodied soup and simmer gently for 8–10 minutes.
Taste and balance salt and tamarind; the dal should suspend evenly rather than settle in a thick mass.
Heat oil over medium heat. Pop mustard seeds, then add dried red chillies and curry leaves and fry for 20–30 seconds.
Pour the sizzling tempering into the sambar, cover immediately, and leave for 5 minutes before stirring.
Serve hot with rice, idli, dosa, or vada; reheat gently because prolonged boiling dulls the fresh tempering.

title: Rasam
description: A practical home-style Rasam recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Crush the tomatoes by hand or with a masher. Combine them with tamarind paste, salt, rasam powder, black pepper, cumin, crushed garlic, and 2 cups water.
Simmer uncovered over medium-low heat for 8–10 minutes, until the tomatoes soften and the raw tamarind smell disappears.
Add the remaining water and bring the rasam only to the point where a foamy cap forms and small bubbles appear at the edge; do not boil vigorously.
Taste and adjust salt and sourness. The broth should be thin, aromatic, peppery, and bright rather than thick like sambar.
Heat oil in a small pan over medium heat and add mustard seeds. When they pop, add curry leaves and fry briefly until crisp.
Pour the tempering over the rasam and switch off the heat immediately.
Cover for 3 minutes to trap the volatile aromas, then add chopped coriander.
Serve steaming in cups or over hot rice, stirring before ladling so the spices are evenly distributed.

title: Kerala Stew
description: A practical home-style Kerala Stew recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 500 g mixed vegetables or chicken pieces
steps: Heat coconut oil in a heavy pot over medium-low heat. Add cinnamon and cloves and fry for 30 seconds until fragrant without darkening.
Add the sliced onion, ginger, and green chillies. Cook for 5–6 minutes until the onion is soft and translucent; it should remain pale.
If using chicken, add it now and turn the pieces for 4–5 minutes until no raw exterior remains. For a vegetable stew, add the firm vegetables first.
Pour in the thin coconut milk, season with salt, cover, and simmer gently until chicken reaches 74°C internally or the vegetables are tender, about 18–25 minutes.
Add quick-cooking vegetables during the final 8–10 minutes so they retain their shape.
Reduce the heat to the lowest setting and stir in the thick coconut milk. Warm for 2–3 minutes, but do not boil or the coconut milk may separate.
Taste for salt and gently crush one piece of potato, if present, to give the broth a little body.
Rest covered for 5 minutes and serve with appam, idiyappam, or bread.

title: Kadala Curry
description: A practical home-style Kadala Curry recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: If starting with dried black chickpeas, soak them for 8–12 hours and pressure-cook with fresh water and salt until tender, about 25–30 minutes. Reserve the cooking liquid.
Dry-roast the grated coconut over medium heat, stirring constantly, until deep golden brown. Add coriander and fennel seeds for the final minute.
Cool the roasted mixture, then grind it with 1/2 cup water to a smooth, dark paste.
Heat coconut oil over medium heat, add curry leaves and onion, and sauté 8–10 minutes until the onion is golden.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, and chilli powder. Cook for 6–8 minutes until the tomatoes collapse and the oil begins to separate.
Stir in the coconut paste and cook for 2 minutes, then add the cooked chickpeas and 1 1/2–2 cups reserved liquid.
Simmer uncovered for 12–15 minutes, crushing a few chickpeas against the side of the pot to thicken the gravy. Adjust salt.
Rest for 10 minutes before serving with puttu or appam; the curry thickens and deepens in flavour as it stands.

title: Bisi Bele Bath
description: A practical home-style Bisi Bele Bath recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the rice and toor dal. Cook them together with turmeric and 4 cups water until extremely soft, about 4 pressure-cooker whistles or 35–40 minutes in a covered pot.
Cook the mixed vegetables separately in lightly salted water until tender but not broken; reserve any cooking liquid.
Dilute the tamarind paste in 1/2 cup warm water. Simmer it for 4–5 minutes, then stir in the bisi bele bath powder.
Add the cooked vegetables, rice-dal mixture, and enough reserved liquid to make a loose, porridge-like consistency. Season with salt.
Simmer over low heat for 8–10 minutes, stirring frequently because the dal and rice catch easily. Add hot water whenever it becomes stiff.
Heat ghee in a small pan. Fry the cashews until golden and remove them; pop mustard seeds, then add curry leaves.
Pour the tempering over the rice, add the fried cashews, and mix thoroughly. The finished dish should be soft, glossy, and spoonable.
Cover and rest for 5 minutes, then serve hot with extra ghee, boondi, or potato chips.

title: Curd Rice
description: A practical home-style Curd Rice recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Cook the rice with extra water until very soft, then mash it while still warm so no firm grains remain.
Cool the mashed rice to lukewarm; hot rice can split the yogurt and make it excessively sour.
Mix in the milk first, then whisk in the yogurt and salt until the texture is creamy and loose. Add a little more milk if needed because it thickens on standing.
Heat oil over medium heat. Add mustard seeds and let them pop, then add urad dal and fry until pale golden.
Add the green chilli, ginger, and curry leaves; fry for 20–30 seconds, taking care as the leaves may splutter.
Pour the tempering over the yogurt-rice mixture and fold it through evenly.
Chill for 20–30 minutes or serve at cool room temperature. If refrigerated longer, loosen with cold milk before serving.
Garnish with pomegranate seeds just before serving so they remain fresh and crisp.

title: Dhokla
description: A practical home-style Dhokla recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Grease a 20 cm steaming tin and bring water to a strong simmer in a covered steamer before mixing the leavening.
Whisk gram flour, yogurt, water, ginger-chilli paste, sugar, salt, and oil for 2 minutes until smooth and free of lumps. Rest for 15 minutes.
Add fruit salt and sprinkle 1 tablespoon water over it. Fold briskly for only 15–20 seconds; the batter should become foamy and lighter.
Immediately pour into the prepared tin and place it in the hot steamer. Steam over medium heat for 15–18 minutes without opening the lid.
Insert a skewer in the centre; it should come out clean. Cool for 5 minutes before cutting into squares.
Heat 1 tablespoon oil, pop the mustard seeds, then add curry leaves and 1/3 cup water. Simmer with a pinch of sugar and salt for 30 seconds.
Pour the warm tempering evenly over the cut dhokla and allow it to absorb for 10 minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve warm or at room temperature with green chutney.

title: Khandvi
description: A practical home-style Khandvi recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk gram flour, yogurt, water, turmeric, ginger-chilli paste, and salt until completely smooth. Strain the mixture into a heavy saucepan.
Grease the backs of several steel trays or a clean stone counter very lightly before cooking, because the batter must be spread immediately.
Cook the batter over medium-low heat for 10–15 minutes, stirring continuously and scraping the base. It is ready when a teaspoon spread thinly sets and peels away cleanly after 30 seconds.
Working quickly, spoon the hot paste onto the prepared surfaces and spread it paper-thin with an offset spatula before it cools.
Leave for 5–7 minutes, then cut into strips about 5 cm wide and roll each strip tightly but gently.
Arrange the rolls seam-side down. Heat oil and add mustard seeds; when they pop, add sesame seeds and curry leaves.
Spoon the hot tempering over the rolls so the sesame seeds adhere to the surface.
Finish with grated coconut and serve at room temperature; properly cooked khandvi should be tender, flexible, and free of raw gram-flour flavour.

title: Undhiyu
description: A practical home-style Undhiyu recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 cups surti papdi or green beans
steps: Make a coarse stuffing by combining coconut, coriander, sesame, ginger-chilli paste, turmeric, jaggery, lemon juice, salt, and 2 tablespoons oil.
Slit the eggplants and potatoes in a cross without cutting through the base. Pack the seasoning firmly into the openings and coat the remaining vegetables with the rest.
Heat the remaining oil in a wide, heavy pot over medium heat. Add the surti papdi or beans and raw banana first, spreading them in an even layer.
Nestle the stuffed potatoes and eggplants above them, add 1/2 cup water, cover tightly, and cook over low heat for 25–35 minutes.
Turn the vegetables only once or twice with a broad spatula so the stuffed pieces remain intact. Add a few tablespoons water if the base dries before the potatoes are tender.
Add the fenugreek dumplings during the last 10–12 minutes, tucking them between the vegetables so they absorb steam without becoming soggy.
Cook uncovered for the final 5 minutes to evaporate excess moisture and allow light caramelisation at the base.
Rest covered for 10 minutes, then serve with puri or rotli; the finished undhiyu should be moist, not gravy-like.

title: Thepla
description: A practical home-style Thepla recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Combine whole-wheat flour, fenugreek leaves, turmeric, chilli powder, cumin, sesame seeds, salt, oil, and yogurt in a bowl.
Rub and squeeze the fenugreek into the flour for 1 minute, then add water gradually and knead for 6–8 minutes to form a soft but non-sticky dough.
Cover and rest for 20 minutes. Divide into equal balls and keep them covered to prevent drying.
Roll each ball on a lightly floured surface into a thin 16–18 cm circle, turning often so it remains even.
Heat a tawa over medium-high heat. Cook one thepla for 30–40 seconds until bubbles appear, then flip.
Brush or drizzle a little oil around the edge and cook for 30 seconds; flip again and press lightly until both sides develop golden spots.
Do not overcook, or the theplas become brittle after cooling. Stack them under a clean towel while finishing the batch.
Serve warm with yogurt or pickle, or cool completely before packing for storage.

title: Handvo
description: A practical home-style Handvo recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Wash the rice and three dals and soak them together for 5–6 hours. Drain and grind to a thick, slightly coarse batter with minimal water.
Mix in yogurt, cover, and ferment in a warm place for 6–8 hours, until lightly risen and tangy.
Fold in grated bottle gourd, ginger-chilli paste, turmeric, sugar, and salt. The batter should be thick enough to drop from a spoon; add a splash of water only if necessary.
Preheat the oven to 180°C and grease a 20 cm heavy baking tin. Heat the oil, pop mustard seeds, then add sesame seeds.
Pour half the tempering into the batter. Add fruit salt immediately before baking and fold gently for 15 seconds.
Pour into the hot prepared tin, level the top, and spoon the remaining tempering over it.
Bake for 35–45 minutes until deeply golden, firm in the centre, and at least 95°C internally; a skewer should emerge clean.
Rest for 10 minutes before cutting. Serve warm with green chutney; the crust should be crisp and the centre moist and porous.

title: Fafda
description: A practical home-style Fafda recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix gram flour, baking soda, carom seeds, turmeric, and salt. Add 2 tablespoons oil and rub it in thoroughly.
Add warm water little by little and knead for 8–10 minutes into a firm, smooth dough. It should be pliable but not soft or sticky.
Cover with a damp cloth and rest for 20–30 minutes, then knead again for 1 minute.
Heat oil in a wide pan to 165–170°C. Pinch off a small ball of dough and place it near the edge of a lightly oiled board.
Using the heel of the palm, drag the dough forward in one firm stroke to make a long, thin strip; lift it with a knife or scraper.
Slide the strip into the oil and fry for 1–2 minutes, turning once, until crisp and pale golden rather than brown.
Drain on a rack and check that the centre snaps cleanly after cooling for a minute. Adjust the dough with a few drops water if strips crack while shaping.
Serve fresh with papaya sambharo and fried green chillies.

title: Sev Tameta Nu Shaak
description: A practical home-style Sev Tameta Nu Shaak recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil over medium heat and add mustard seeds; when they pop, add cumin seeds and let them sizzle for a few seconds.
Add the chopped tomatoes, turmeric, chilli powder, coriander powder, and salt. Stir well and cook covered for 6–8 minutes.
Mash the softened tomatoes with the back of a spoon until they form a coarse sauce, leaving a few small pieces for texture.
Add the jaggery and water. Simmer uncovered for 5–7 minutes until the gravy is glossy and balances sweet, sour, and spicy flavours.
Taste before adding the sev because its saltiness varies. The sauce should be slightly looser than the desired final consistency.
Turn the heat to low and add three-quarters of the sev, folding it in gently. Simmer for only 30–60 seconds.
Remove from the heat immediately; prolonged cooking makes the sev dissolve completely.
Serve at once, topped with the remaining crisp sev, alongside rotli or bhakri.

title: Gujarati Kadhi
description: A practical home-style Gujarati Kadhi recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk yogurt and gram flour for 2 minutes until perfectly smooth, then whisk in water, turmeric, ginger, green chillies, sugar or jaggery, and salt.
Pour into a heavy pot and bring to a gentle boil over medium heat, whisking frequently during the first 8–10 minutes so the mixture does not split or catch.
Lower the heat and simmer uncovered for 15–20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the raw gram-flour taste disappears and the kadhi is lightly thickened.
Taste and balance the characteristic sweet-sour profile with a little more sugar or salt if needed.
Heat ghee in a small pan over medium heat. Add mustard seeds, then cumin seeds, and let both crackle.
Add dried red chillies and curry leaves and fry for 20 seconds until aromatic.
Pour the tempering into the simmering kadhi, stir once, and cook for 1 final minute.
Serve hot with plain rice or khichdi; keep the consistency pourable because it thickens as it cools.

title: Khichu
description: A practical home-style Khichu recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: Sesame seeds and chilli oil for serving
steps: Bring the water, cumin seeds, green chilli paste, salt, and 1 tablespoon oil to a rolling boil in a heavy saucepan.
Mix the baking soda into the rice flour. Reduce the heat to low and add the flour gradually while stirring vigorously with a sturdy wooden spoon.
Continue stirring and pressing for 2–3 minutes until no dry flour remains and a thick, sticky mass forms.
Cover tightly and steam-cook on the lowest heat for 8–10 minutes, stirring once halfway through to prevent scorching.
Test a small portion; it should taste fully cooked and no longer chalky. Add 1–2 tablespoons boiling water if it seems dry before the flour is cooked.
Turn off the heat and beat the dough firmly for 1 minute to smooth out remaining lumps.
Spoon into bowls while piping hot and make a shallow well in the centre.
Drizzle with the remaining oil and finish with sesame seeds and chilli oil just before eating.

title: Patra
description: A practical home-style Patra recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Wash and dry the colocasia leaves. Trim the thick central ribs flush with the leaf using a sharp knife so the leaves roll without splitting.
Mix gram flour, tamarind, jaggery, chilli powder, turmeric, coriander-cumin powder, sesame seeds, salt, and enough water to make a thick, spreadable paste.
Place the largest leaf vein-side up and spread a thin layer of paste over it. Stack a second leaf in the opposite direction and repeat, using 3–4 leaves per roll.
Fold the sides inward, then roll tightly from the broad end toward the tip. Secure the seam with a little paste.
Steam the rolls over actively simmering water for 20–25 minutes, until firm and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean.
Cool completely for at least 20 minutes before slicing into 1 cm rounds; warm rolls tear easily.
Heat oil, pop mustard seeds, and sauté the slices in a single layer for 2–3 minutes per side until lightly crisp at the edges.
Serve warm, showing the spiral layers, with chutney or a squeeze of lemon.

title: Dal Baati
description: A practical home-style Dal Baati recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the mixed dals and pressure-cook with turmeric and enough water for 4 whistles, or simmer until very soft. Whisk lightly so some lentils break down.
Heat 1 tablespoon ghee, add cumin, then onion and cook until golden. Add tomatoes and cook until pulpy; stir this masala into the dal, season, and simmer for 10 minutes.
For the baati, combine whole-wheat flour, semolina, salt, and 1/2 cup ghee. Rub until the mixture holds when squeezed, then add water gradually to make a firm dough.
Knead only 3–4 minutes, cover, and rest for 20 minutes. Divide into smooth balls and make a shallow thumb indentation in each.
Preheat the oven to 200°C. Arrange the baati on a tray and bake for 25–35 minutes, turning once, until deep golden and cooked through.
Crack one open to check that the centre is dry and layered, not doughy. Dip each hot baati briefly in melted ghee or brush generously.
Reheat the dal and adjust it to a pourable consistency. Finish with the remaining hot ghee and, if desired, extra cumin tempering.
Crush the baati in serving bowls, ladle dal over it, and serve immediately with churma and pickle.

title: Churma
description: A practical home-style Churma recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Combine whole-wheat flour and semolina. Rub in half the ghee until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs and holds together when pressed.
Add warm milk or water gradually and form a stiff dough without prolonged kneading. Rest covered for 15 minutes.
Divide into thick, flattened portions and bake at 180°C for 25–30 minutes, turning once, until deeply golden and completely dry inside; alternatively fry slowly in ghee.
Cool until safe to handle, break into pieces, and grind to coarse crumbs rather than a fine powder.
Pass through a wide sieve and regrind only the larger pieces for an even, sandy texture.
Warm the remaining ghee and mix it thoroughly into the crumbs. Let the mixture cool below 40°C before adding sugar so it does not melt.
Fold in powdered sugar or finely grated jaggery, cardamom, and chopped nuts, rubbing with the fingertips to distribute evenly.
Serve loose or press into small balls; store only after it has cooled completely.

title: Gatte Ki Sabzi
description: A practical home-style Gatte Ki Sabzi recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mix 2 cups gram flour with carom seeds, chilli powder, turmeric, salt, 2 tablespoons oil, and 1/2 cup yogurt. Knead into a firm, smooth dough, adding only a spoonful of water if essential.
Divide into finger-thick logs. Lower them into a wide pot of gently boiling salted water and cook for 10–12 minutes, until they float and feel firm.
Remove the logs, reserve 2 cups cooking water, and cool for 5 minutes before slicing into 1.5 cm rounds.
Whisk the remaining yogurt with coriander powder, cumin, and a little reserved water until smooth.
Heat 3 tablespoons oil over medium-low heat, add the yogurt mixture gradually while stirring continuously, and cook for 6–8 minutes until glossy.
Add the sliced gatte and enough reserved cooking water for a medium gravy. Simmer gently for 10 minutes so the dumplings absorb flavour.
Adjust salt and consistency; the gravy should coat the gatte but remain spoonable.
Rest for 10 minutes before serving with roti or rice, as the dumplings continue to soften in the sauce.

title: Ker Sangri
description: A practical home-style Ker Sangri recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the dried ker and sangri thoroughly, then soak them separately in plenty of water for 8 hours or overnight.
Boil the soaked ingredients in fresh salted water for 12–15 minutes until tender but not mushy. Drain, rinse once, and squeeze out excess water.
Heat oil in a heavy pan over medium heat. Add cumin and fennel and let them sizzle for 20 seconds.
Add coriander powder, chilli powder, and turmeric with 1 tablespoon water so the spices do not burn.
Add the ker and sangri and sauté for 5–6 minutes, turning gently until coated and lightly fried.
Lower the heat, stir in the yogurt a spoonful at a time, and cook until absorbed and the oil reappears.
Add dried mango powder and salt, then cook uncovered for another 3–4 minutes until the mixture is dry and glossy.
Cool for 5 minutes before serving; the dish should be tangy, concentrated, and free of excess liquid.

title: Panchmel Dal
description: A practical home-style Panchmel Dal recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1/4 cup each moong, masoor, chana, toor and urad dal
steps: Pick over and rinse all five dals until the water runs clear. Soak for 30 minutes, then drain.
Cook the dals with turmeric and 4 cups water until tender but not completely puréed, about 4 pressure-cooker whistles or 35 minutes in a covered pot.
Heat ghee over medium heat and add cumin seeds. When aromatic, add onion and cook for 7–8 minutes until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and fry for 1 minute, then add tomatoes, coriander powder, and chilli powder.
Cook the masala for 6–8 minutes, mashing the tomatoes, until the ghee separates at the edges.
Add the cooked dals and salt, then simmer uncovered for 10–12 minutes. Stir firmly once or twice to blend the varieties while retaining some texture.
Adjust with hot water to a medium-thick consistency and taste for seasoning.
Rest covered for 5 minutes and serve with baati, rice, or roti, finished with an extra spoonful of hot ghee if desired.

title: Laal Maas
description: A practical home-style Laal Maas recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 8 dried Mathania or mild red chillies, soaked
steps: Drain the soaked red chillies and blend them with a little soaking water into a smooth, vivid paste.
Heat ghee in a heavy pot over medium heat. Add cumin, cloves, cardamom, and cinnamon and fry for 30 seconds.
Add the sliced onions and cook for 10–12 minutes until deep golden. Stir in ginger-garlic paste and fry for 1 minute.
Add the mutton and sear over medium-high heat for 8–10 minutes, turning until the pieces lose their raw colour and begin to brown.
Lower the heat and stir in the chilli paste. Beat the yogurt until smooth and add it gradually, stirring continuously so it does not split.
Cook the masala for 8–10 minutes until the ghee separates, then add 2 cups hot water and salt. Cover and simmer gently for 60–90 minutes until the meat is fork-tender.
Uncover and reduce the gravy to a glossy, medium consistency. The mutton should reach at least 74°C and pull cleanly from the bone.
Rest covered for 10 minutes before serving with bajra roti or steamed rice.

title: Safed Maas
description: A practical home-style Safed Maas recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the cashews and poppy seeds in hot water for 20 minutes, then blend with 1/4 cup water to a very smooth paste.
Heat ghee over medium-low heat and add cardamom and cinnamon. Add the onions and cook slowly for 8–10 minutes until soft and pale, not browned.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute, then add the mutton and sear for 7–8 minutes without colouring the onions excessively.
Whisk the yogurt until smooth and add it in several portions over low heat, stirring after each addition.
Add the nut-poppy paste, white pepper, salt, and 1 1/2 cups hot water. Cover and simmer very gently for 60–90 minutes until the meat is tender.
Stir occasionally and add small amounts of hot water if the nut paste begins to stick before the meat softens.
When the mutton is tender and at least 74°C internally, stir in the cream and warm for 3–4 minutes without a hard boil.
Adjust salt and rest for 10 minutes; the final gravy should be pale, rich, smooth, and mildly spiced.

title: Bajra Khichdi
description: A practical home-style Bajra Khichdi recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Drain the soaked pearl millet and pulse it briefly in a processor or crush in a mortar to split some grains; this helps it cook evenly.
Rinse the millet and moong dal together, then place in a pressure cooker with turmeric, salt, and 5 cups water.
Pressure-cook for 6–7 whistles, or simmer in a heavy covered pot for 60–75 minutes, until the millet is fully tender and the dal has broken down.
Allow pressure to release naturally, then stir vigorously. Add boiling water if needed to achieve a thick porridge consistency.
Heat ghee over medium heat and add cumin seeds, allowing them to darken slightly and become fragrant.
Pour the hot cumin ghee over the khichdi and mix thoroughly.
Simmer uncovered for 3–4 minutes, stirring frequently, and correct the salt.
Finish with coriander and serve very hot with extra ghee, yogurt, or kadhi.

title: Papad Ki Sabzi
description: A practical home-style Papad Ki Sabzi recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Roast the papads over an open flame or on a dry tawa until crisp and blistered, then break them into large pieces.
Whisk yogurt, gram flour, turmeric, chilli powder, coriander powder, and 1/2 cup water until smooth.
Heat oil over medium heat and add cumin seeds. Once they sizzle, reduce the heat to low.
Pour in the yogurt mixture slowly while stirring continuously. Cook for 5–6 minutes until it comes to a gentle simmer and loses its raw gram-flour taste.
Add the remaining water and salt, then simmer for 5 minutes to form a light, tangy gravy.
Add the roasted papad pieces and press them beneath the gravy. Cook for only 1–2 minutes, depending on how soft you prefer them.
Remove from the heat before the papads disintegrate; they continue absorbing liquid as the curry stands.
Serve immediately with roti, keeping extra roasted papad aside for a crisp topping if desired.

title: Moong Dal Pakodi
description: A practical home-style Moong Dal Pakodi recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 1/2 cups split yellow moong dal, soaked 3 hours
steps: Drain the soaked moong dal thoroughly. Reserve 2 tablespoons whole dal and grind the remainder with very little water to a coarse, thick paste.
Transfer to a bowl and beat vigorously for 2–3 minutes to incorporate air. Fold in the reserved dal, onion, green chillies, cumin, chilli powder, coriander, and salt.
Rest the batter for 10 minutes. It should hold a rough mound on a spoon; if watery, add 1 tablespoon gram flour rather than more dal water.
Heat 5–7 cm oil in a deep pan to 170–175°C.
Drop small, irregular teaspoonfuls into the oil, leaving enough room for them to expand and move freely.
Fry for 4–5 minutes, turning several times, until crisp, evenly golden, and cooked through at the centre.
Drain on a rack and allow the oil to return to temperature before frying the next batch.
Serve hot with green chutney, or soak in hot water and squeeze gently if using the pakodis in a yogurt-based preparation.

title: Chole Bhature
description: A practical home-style Chole Bhature recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: For the bhature dough, combine all-purpose flour, baking powder, sugar, and salt. Add yogurt and 2 tablespoons oil, then knead with enough warm water for 8–10 minutes until soft, smooth, and slightly tacky.
Cover the dough and rest it in a warm place for at least 2 hours; a 4-hour rest gives better puffing and a light fermented flavour.
For the chole, heat 3 tablespoons oil over medium heat. Sauté the onion for 8–10 minutes until deep golden, then add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes and chole masala and cook for 7–9 minutes, mashing the tomatoes, until the oil separates. Add chickpeas, dried mango powder, salt, and 1 1/2 cups water.
Simmer uncovered for 15–20 minutes, crushing about one-quarter of the chickpeas to thicken the gravy. It should be dark, glossy, and spoon-coating.
Divide the rested dough into balls. Roll each to a 14–16 cm oval, keeping the centre slightly thicker than the edges.
Heat frying oil to 185°C. Slide in one bhatura, press the surface gently with a slotted spoon until it balloons, then turn and fry for 30–45 seconds more until pale golden.
Drain and serve immediately with the hot chole; bhature lose their characteristic puff if held for long.

title: Rajma Chawal
description: A practical home-style Rajma Chawal recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: If using dried kidney beans, soak them for 8–12 hours, drain, and pressure-cook in fresh water until completely tender, about 25–35 minutes. A bean should crush easily between two fingers.
Rinse the basmati rice until clear, soak for 20 minutes, then cook with measured salted water until the grains are tender and separate. Rest covered for 10 minutes and fluff.
Heat oil over medium heat, add cumin, and let it sizzle. Add onion and cook for 8–10 minutes until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste for 1 minute, then tomato purée, coriander powder, turmeric, and salt. Cook 8–10 minutes until the masala is thick and the oil shows at the edges.
Add the cooked rajma and 2 cups of its cooking liquid. Simmer gently for 20–25 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Mash a small ladleful of beans against the side of the pot and stir it back in to create the traditional creamy body without added cream.
Add garam masala and simmer for 3 final minutes. Rest the rajma for 10 minutes so the gravy settles and thickens.
Spoon rajma over hot rice and serve; the beans must be fully soft because undercooked kidney beans are unsafe and unpleasant.

title: Palak Paneer
description: A practical home-style Palak Paneer recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bring a large pot of water to a boil. Add the spinach and blanch for 60–90 seconds, then transfer immediately to ice water to preserve its colour.
Drain thoroughly and blend with the green chilli and only enough water to form a smooth but not watery purée.
Heat 1 tablespoon oil in a pan and sear the paneer cubes over medium heat for 1–2 minutes per side until lightly golden. Remove; do not overcook or they become rubbery.
Add the remaining oil and cumin to the pan. Add onion and sauté for 7–8 minutes until golden, then add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute.
Add tomatoes and salt and cook for 6–8 minutes until pulpy and the oil separates.
Stir in the spinach purée and simmer uncovered over low heat for 5–7 minutes. Avoid prolonged high heat, which darkens the greens.
Add paneer, garam masala, and cream. Simmer very gently for 2–3 minutes, just long enough to heat the paneer through.
Rest for 5 minutes and serve; the gravy should be vivid green, smooth, and thick enough to cling to the paneer.

title: Shahi Paneer
description: A practical home-style Shahi Paneer recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the cashews in hot water for 20 minutes. Meanwhile, heat half the ghee and cook the sliced onions over medium-low heat for 10–12 minutes until soft and only lightly golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute, then cool slightly and blend the onion mixture with the soaked cashews and enough water to make a very smooth paste.
Heat the remaining ghee over low heat. Add cardamom and cinnamon and fry for 30 seconds.
Add the onion-cashew paste and cook for 6–8 minutes, stirring constantly, until it becomes glossy and loses its raw aroma.
Whisk the yogurt until smooth. Lower the heat and add it a spoonful at a time, stirring continuously; simmer gently until the ghee begins to separate.
Add 3/4 cup hot water, salt, and garam masala. Simmer for 5 minutes, then add the paneer and cook for only 2 minutes.
Stir in the cream and warm without boiling. Remove the whole spices if desired.
Serve after a 5-minute rest; the sauce should be pale, silky, mildly spiced, and rich rather than tomato-red.

title: Paneer Butter Masala
description: A practical home-style Paneer Butter Masala recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Soak the cashews in hot water for 15 minutes. Blend them with the tomato purée until very smooth.
Melt 2 tablespoons butter over medium heat. Add the onion and cook for 6–8 minutes until soft, then add ginger-garlic paste and fry for 1 minute.
Add chilli powder and the tomato-cashew purée. Cook uncovered for 10–12 minutes, stirring frequently, until thick, glossy, and no longer raw-smelling.
Pass the sauce through a sieve for a restaurant-style texture, then return it to the pan with 3/4 cup hot water and salt.
Crush the dried fenugreek between the palms and add it with garam masala. Simmer on low for 5 minutes.
Add paneer cubes and cook very gently for 2–3 minutes; avoid a hard boil so the paneer stays tender.
Stir in the cream and remaining butter, then switch off the heat as soon as the sauce is evenly emulsified.
Rest for 5 minutes before serving; the finished gravy should be smooth, buttery, mildly sweet from the tomatoes, and thick enough to coat each cube.

title: Malai Kofta
description: A practical home-style Malai Kofta recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Mash the boiled potatoes until completely smooth, then mix with grated paneer, cornstarch, a pinch of salt, and the chopped raisins and cashews.
Divide into equal portions and shape into crack-free balls. Chill for 20 minutes so they hold their shape during frying.
Heat oil to 170–175°C and fry one test kofta. If it breaks, knead 1 teaspoon more cornstarch into the mixture. Fry the remaining koftas until evenly golden and drain.
For the gravy, heat 3 tablespoons oil and cook the onions for 8–10 minutes until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste for 1 minute, then tomatoes and cook until fully collapsed.
Blend the cooked masala with cashews and 1/2 cup water until smooth; strain if desired, then return it to the pan.
Add garam masala, salt, and enough hot water for a medium-thick gravy. Simmer for 8 minutes, then stir in the cream and warm gently.
Place the fried koftas in the serving dish and pour the hot gravy over them only 3–5 minutes before serving; longer soaking makes them fragile.
Serve immediately, ensuring the koftas remain soft inside with a lightly fried exterior.

title: Sarson Saag
description: A practical home-style Sarson Saag recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 750 g spinach, mustard greens or mixed leafy greens
steps: Wash the greens in several changes of water to remove grit, then chop them roughly, including tender stems.
Place the greens, green chillies, turmeric, salt, and 1 cup water in a heavy pot. Cover and simmer for 35–45 minutes, stirring occasionally, until completely tender.
Cool slightly and blend or churn to a coarse purée; traditional saag should retain some texture rather than become perfectly smooth.
Return the greens to low heat and simmer uncovered for 10–15 minutes, stirring frequently, until thick and concentrated.
Heat oil or ghee in a separate pan, add cumin, then onion. Cook for 8 minutes until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 1 minute, then add tomatoes and coriander powder and cook until the tomatoes are jammy and the fat separates.
Stir the tempering into the greens and simmer together for another 8–10 minutes. Adjust salt and consistency.
Finish with a spoon of ghee and serve hot with makki ki roti; the saag should mound softly on a spoon, not run like soup.

title: Amritsari Fish
description: A practical home-style Amritsari Fish recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Pat the fish completely dry and cut it into even pieces. Toss with lemon juice, ginger-garlic paste, salt, chilli powder, turmeric, coriander powder, and carom seeds; marinate 20 minutes under refrigeration.
Whisk gram flour and rice flour with about 3/4 cup water to make a thick, smooth batter that coats the back of a spoon.
Fold the marinated fish and any released juices into the batter so every piece is evenly coated. Rest for 10 minutes.
Heat 5–7 cm oil to 175–180°C. Shake excess batter from each piece before lowering it into the oil.
Fry in small batches for 3–5 minutes, depending on thickness, turning once or twice until crisp and deep golden.
The fish is done when it flakes easily and reaches 63°C at the thickest point. Do not crowd the pan, which lowers the oil temperature and softens the coating.
Drain on a wire rack rather than stacking on paper, and let the oil return to temperature between batches.
Serve immediately with lemon and chutney while the gram-flour crust is still crisp.

title: Paneer Tikka
description: A practical home-style Paneer Tikka recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk thick yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, garam masala, oil, and salt until smooth.
Add paneer, bell pepper, and onion and fold gently until coated. Refrigerate for 30–60 minutes; longer marination can make paneer crumbly.
If using wooden skewers, soak them for 30 minutes. Thread paneer and vegetables alternately, leaving small gaps for heat circulation.
Preheat the oven to 230°C with the grill or broiler element and place a rack over a lined tray.
Arrange the skewers on the rack and roast for 8 minutes. Turn, brush lightly with remaining marinade or oil, and cook 5–7 minutes more.
Broil for the final 1–2 minutes until the edges char in spots, watching closely to prevent the paneer from drying.
Rest for 2 minutes and slide the tikka from the skewers. The paneer should be hot and tender, with lightly blistered vegetables.
Serve with mint chutney, lemon, and sliced onion.

title: Tandoori Paneer
description: A practical home-style Tandoori Paneer recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Press the paneer gently between paper towels for 10 minutes so the marinade adheres, then cut it into large 3 cm cubes.
Whisk yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, garam masala, oil, and salt into a thick, highly seasoned marinade.
Coat paneer, onion, and bell pepper carefully and refrigerate for 45 minutes. Bring to cool room temperature for 10 minutes before cooking.
Preheat a grill, tandoor, or oven with a broiler to its highest setting, ideally 240–250°C. Thread the pieces onto metal skewers.
Cook 5–6 minutes close to the heat source, then rotate the skewers and cook another 4–5 minutes.
Brush with a trace of oil and expose to direct high heat for 30–60 seconds per side to create characteristic smoky blistering.
Remove as soon as the paneer is heated through; prolonged cooking squeezes out moisture and toughens it.
Sprinkle with lemon juice and serve immediately from the skewers with green chutney.

title: Pav Bhaji
description: A practical home-style Pav Bhaji recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Boil or steam the potatoes, cauliflower, and peas until very soft. Drain, reserving 1 cup cooking liquid, and mash the vegetables coarsely.
Melt 2 tablespoons butter with the oil on a wide tawa or heavy pan. Cook one chopped onion for 7–8 minutes until translucent.
Add bell pepper and sauté for 4 minutes, then add tomatoes, salt, and pav bhaji masala. Cook 8–10 minutes, mashing continuously, until thick and glossy.
Add the cooked vegetables and 1/2 cup reserved liquid. Mash vigorously with a potato masher for 8–10 minutes over medium heat.
Add more reserved liquid as needed and simmer for 10 minutes, stirring frequently. The bhaji should be cohesive, soft, and spoonable, not watery.
Mix in 1 tablespoon butter and lemon juice, then adjust salt and spice. Keep hot on very low heat.
Split the pav without cutting fully through. Toast cut-side down in the remaining butter on a hot tawa for 1–2 minutes until golden.
Serve bhaji topped with butter, coriander, and the remaining chopped onion, with lemon and hot toasted pav.

title: Vada Pav
description: A practical home-style Vada Pav recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat 1 tablespoon oil and pop mustard seeds. Add curry leaves and ginger-garlic-chilli paste and fry for 30 seconds.
Add turmeric and the boiled potatoes with salt. Mash and fold over medium heat for 3–4 minutes, then cool and shape into compact balls.
Whisk gram flour, salt, and water into a smooth, thick batter that falls slowly from a spoon. Rest for 10 minutes.
Heat frying oil to 175°C. Dip each potato ball completely in batter, allow excess to drip, and slide it into the oil.
Fry for 3–4 minutes, turning until evenly golden and crisp. Drain on a rack.
Split each pav and spread dry garlic chutney generously on the cut surfaces.
Place one hot vada inside, press gently, and serve at once. Fried green chillies may be tucked alongside.
Keep the potato filling warm and fry only as needed so the contrast between crisp coating and soft centre is preserved.

title: Misal Pav
description: A practical home-style Misal Pav recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat oil over medium heat and sauté the onion for 7–8 minutes until golden. Add tomatoes and cook until pulpy.
Add misal masala and chilli powder with 2 tablespoons water; fry for 2–3 minutes until fragrant and the oil separates.
Add the cooked sprouted moth beans, salt, and 2 cups water. Simmer for 12–15 minutes so the sprouts absorb the masala.
For a proper tarri, add the remaining water and simmer uncovered until a layer of spiced red oil rises to the surface. Adjust salt and heat.
Spoon the sprout mixture into deep bowls and ladle over enough hot tarri to make it soupy.
Top each bowl with farsan immediately before serving so some remains crunchy while some softens in the gravy.
Finish with coriander, chopped onion if available, and a squeeze of lemon.
Serve with warm pav on the side; diners can add more tarri according to their preferred heat.

title: Puran Poli
description: A practical home-style Puran Poli recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Rinse the chana dal and cook it in plenty of water until tender but still holding shape, about 20–25 minutes. Drain very thoroughly.
Cook the drained dal and jaggery together over medium-low heat for 10–15 minutes, stirring and mashing, until the mixture becomes thick and leaves the sides of the pan.
Add cardamom and nutmeg, cool completely, then grind or mash to a perfectly smooth filling. Divide into balls.
Mix flour and oil, then knead with water for 8 minutes into a very soft, elastic dough. Coat lightly with oil, cover, and rest for 1 hour.
Take a small dough portion, flatten it, and enclose a larger ball of puran. Pinch the seam tightly and flatten gently.
Roll with light pressure into a thin 18–20 cm circle, using flour sparingly so the filling stays evenly distributed.
Cook on a medium-hot tawa for 30–45 seconds per side, then apply ghee and cook until both sides show golden spots.
Serve warm with extra ghee or milk; the poli should be thin, soft, and filled edge to edge.

title: Bharli Vangi
description: A practical home-style Bharli Vangi recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Dry-grind the roasted peanuts, coconut, and sesame to a coarse powder. Mix with onion, goda masala, chilli powder, turmeric, jaggery, tamarind, and salt.
Wash the eggplants and cut each in a deep cross from the base, leaving the stem end intact. Pack the stuffing firmly into every slit.
Heat oil in a wide heavy pan over medium heat and arrange the stuffed eggplants in a single layer. Sauté gently for 3–4 minutes, turning once.
Scatter any remaining stuffing around them, add the water, and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cover and cook over low heat for 20–30 minutes, turning the eggplants carefully every 7–8 minutes so they cook evenly without breaking.
Uncover when a knife slides easily into the thickest part. Reduce the gravy for 5 minutes until the nut mixture is glossy and clings to the eggplants.
Taste and balance the sweet-sour seasoning with jaggery, tamarind, or salt.
Rest covered for 10 minutes before serving with bhakri or chapati.

title: Sabudana Khichdi
description: A practical home-style Sabudana Khichdi recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 2 cups sabudana, rinsed and soaked 5 hours
steps: After soaking, press a pearl of sabudana between the fingers; it should mash easily with no hard centre. Drain off every trace of free water.
Toss the soaked sabudana with coarsely ground peanuts, salt, and sugar, separating any clumps with the fingers.
Heat ghee in a wide non-stick or seasoned pan over medium heat. Add cumin and green chillies and fry for 20 seconds.
Add the potato cubes and sauté for 5–7 minutes until lightly golden and fully tender.
Add the sabudana mixture and toss gently over medium heat for 4–6 minutes. Do not cover, which traps steam and makes it sticky.
Cook only until most pearls turn translucent; overcooking causes them to fuse into a rubbery mass.
Remove from the heat, add lemon juice and coriander, and toss once more.
Serve immediately while the pearls are separate, soft, and lightly coated with peanut.

title: Thalipeeth
description: A practical home-style Thalipeeth recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Combine thalipeeth flour, onion, green chillies, sesame, cumin, turmeric, coriander, and salt.
Add water gradually and mix into a soft, moist dough. Rest for 10 minutes so the grains hydrate.
Place a portion on a wet cloth, banana leaf, or lightly oiled sheet and pat with damp fingers into a 15 cm round about 4 mm thick.
Make 4–5 small holes through the round; these help oil reach the centre and promote even cooking.
Invert onto a medium-hot tawa and peel away the cloth or sheet. Drizzle oil around the edge and into the holes.
Cover and cook for 2–3 minutes until the base is browned and the top looks set.
Flip carefully and cook uncovered for another 2 minutes, pressing gently until both sides are crisp in spots and the centre is cooked.
Serve hot with yogurt, butter, or pickle.

title: Zunka Bhakar
description: A practical home-style Zunka Bhakar recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 4 jowar or bajra bhakri for serving
steps: Whisk the gram flour with 1 cup water until smooth, then whisk in the remaining water and salt to prevent lumps during cooking.
Heat oil in a wide pan over medium heat. Pop mustard seeds, add cumin, then onion and green chillies.
Cook the onion for 6–7 minutes until soft and lightly golden, then add turmeric.
Reduce the heat and pour in the gram-flour slurry in a steady stream while stirring continuously.
Cook for 8–12 minutes, stirring and scraping the base, until the mixture thickens from a batter into moist crumbs and no raw gram-flour aroma remains.
For a drier zunka, continue cooking uncovered for 3–5 minutes, breaking it into small granules with the spatula.
Taste for salt and rest off the heat for 3 minutes; it will firm further as steam escapes.
Serve hot with jowar or bajra bhakri, raw onion, and green chilli.

title: Batata Vada
description: A practical home-style Batata Vada recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Heat 1 tablespoon oil, pop mustard seeds, then add curry leaves and ginger-green chilli paste. Fry for 30–45 seconds.
Add turmeric and mashed potatoes and season with salt. Cook for 3 minutes, then add lemon juice and cool completely.
Shape the filling into smooth balls, compressing them enough to prevent cracking in the batter.
Whisk gram flour, rice flour, chilli powder, salt, and water into a thick, lump-free batter. Rest for 10 minutes.
Heat oil to 175°C. Dip each potato ball in batter, coat completely, and lower gently into the oil.
Fry in batches for 3–4 minutes, turning until crisp and evenly golden. The rice flour should create a light shell rather than a thick doughy layer.
Drain on a rack and allow the oil to recover to temperature before the next batch.
Serve hot with chutney, or place inside pav to make vada pav.

title: Kolhapuri Chicken
description: A practical home-style Kolhapuri Chicken recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
ingredients: 1 tsp paprika or red chilli powder
steps: Mix yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, garam masala, salt, and 1 tablespoon oil. Coat the chicken and refrigerate for 1–2 hours.
Heat the remaining oil in a heavy pan over medium-high heat. Lift the chicken from the marinade and sear it in batches for 3–4 minutes per side until lightly browned.
Return all chicken to the pan and add the remaining marinade with 1/2 cup hot water, scraping up the browned bits.
Cover and simmer over medium-low heat for 20–25 minutes, turning the pieces once, until nearly tender.
Uncover and cook for 8–10 minutes, stirring frequently, until the sauce reduces and clings to the chicken.
Check that the thickest pieces reach at least 74°C and the juices run clear.
Taste and adjust salt and chilli; Kolhapuri chicken should be robust and hot, with concentrated masala rather than a thin gravy.
Rest covered for 5 minutes before serving with bhakri or rice.

title: Mysore Pak
description: A practical home-style Mysore Pak recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Sift the gram flour. Warm the ghee in a saucepan and keep it hot but not smoking over the lowest heat.
Grease and line a square tin before starting; once the mixture reaches the correct stage it must be poured immediately.
Boil sugar and water over medium heat until the syrup reaches one-string consistency, about 110–112°C.
Reduce the heat and add the gram flour gradually, whisking continuously until completely smooth and free of dry pockets.
Add the hot ghee one ladle at a time, stirring vigorously after each addition. Wait until each portion is absorbed before adding the next.
Continue cooking and stirring until the mixture becomes porous, foamy, and leaves the pan while releasing ghee at the edges, about 12–18 minutes.
Immediately pour into the tin without pressing down; level only lightly so the aerated structure remains. Sprinkle cardamom if using.
Cool 15–20 minutes, cut while warm, and allow to set fully before separating the pieces.

title: Kaju Katli
description: A practical home-style Kaju Katli recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Chill the cashews for 20 minutes, then grind in short pulses to a fine powder. Do not overprocess or the nuts will release oil.
Sift the cashew powder to remove coarse particles. Grease two sheets of parchment or reusable silicone lightly with ghee.
Cook sugar and water over medium heat until the syrup reaches one-string consistency, about 110–112°C.
Reduce the heat and add the cashew powder gradually, stirring constantly with a sturdy spatula.
Cook for 5–8 minutes until the mixture forms a soft mass and a small cooled piece rolls into a non-sticky ball. Add cardamom.
Turn onto parchment and let it cool just enough to handle, then knead briefly with greased hands until smooth. Do not knead after it becomes cold.
Place the second sheet on top and roll evenly to 4–5 mm thickness. Garnish with finely chopped pistachios if desired.
Cool completely and cut into diamonds with a greased knife.

title: Sandesh
description: A practical home-style Sandesh recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Bring the milk to a full boil, lower the heat, and add diluted lemon juice gradually while stirring until the curds separate from pale green whey.
Pour through a muslin-lined strainer and rinse the chhena under cool water to remove lemon flavour. Gather and hang for 30–45 minutes; it should remain moist, not dry.
Turn the chhena onto a clean plate and knead with the heel of the hand for 8–10 minutes until completely smooth and slightly glossy.
Mix in powdered sugar and cardamom. Transfer to a non-stick pan over the lowest heat.
Cook for 4–6 minutes, stirring constantly, only until the mixture loses excess moisture and gathers softly. Overcooking makes sandesh grainy and hard.
Cool until warm, then knead for another minute and divide into portions.
Shape into discs or press into moulds and garnish each with pistachio.
Chill for 30 minutes before serving, but bring close to room temperature for the best delicate texture.

title: Peda
description: A practical home-style Peda recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Grease a heavy non-stick pan with the ghee and place it over low heat.
Add condensed milk and milk powder and whisk thoroughly before the pan becomes hot, eliminating all dry lumps.
Cook over low heat for 8–12 minutes, stirring and scraping continuously, until the mixture thickens into a soft dough and leaves the sides.
Add cardamom and continue cooking for 1 minute. A small cooled portion should hold a ball without sticking heavily.
Transfer to a greased plate and cool until warm enough to handle; do not refrigerate the hot mixture.
Grease the palms lightly and knead for 30 seconds, then divide into equal portions and roll smooth.
Flatten each ball gently, make a thumb impression, and press pistachios into the centre.
Let the peda set at room temperature for 1 hour before storing in an airtight container under refrigeration.

title: Motichoor Ladoo
description: A practical home-style Motichoor Ladoo recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk gram flour with enough water to make a smooth, flowing batter slightly thinner than ordinary boondi batter; add orange colour if desired. Rest for 10 minutes.
Cook sugar and 3/4 cup water to a sticky one-string syrup, about 108–110°C. Add cardamom and keep warm over very low heat.
Heat oil or ghee to 165–170°C. Hold a fine-hole motichoor jhara 5–7 cm above the oil and rub or tap small amounts of batter through it.
Fry the tiny droplets for only 30–40 seconds; they should cook but remain soft and bright, not crisp or brown.
Drain each batch and place it directly into the warm syrup. Repeat, cleaning the jhara between batches so the droplets remain fine.
When all boondi is added, mix in melon seeds and let it absorb syrup for 10 minutes. Pulse very briefly if the grains need slight binding, without making a paste.
While still warm, press firmly into compact round ladoos with greased hands.
Leave uncovered for 30 minutes to set before storing.

title: Besan Ladoo
description: A practical home-style Besan Ladoo recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Melt the ghee in a heavy pan over low heat. Add the gram flour and mix until every particle is coated.
Roast over low to medium-low heat for 20–30 minutes, stirring and scraping constantly. The flour will first thicken, then loosen and become aromatic and golden.
Continue until the raw smell is completely gone and the mixture looks glossy, but do not let it darken rapidly or scorch.
Remove from the heat and spread in a wide bowl. Cool to lukewarm, below about 40°C, so the sugar will not melt.
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, and chopped nuts and rub together thoroughly until evenly distributed.
Squeeze a portion in the fist; if it will not hold, mix in 1–2 teaspoons warm ghee rather than adding liquid.
Roll firmly into smooth ladoos and press a nut into each if desired.
Allow to firm at room temperature for 1 hour before storing airtight.

title: Boondi Ladoo
description: A practical home-style Boondi Ladoo recipe from Indian cuisine
licenseNote: Editorial home-kitchen formulation generated for this catalogue; test and review for local ingredient standards, nutrition, food safety, and allergen compliance before commercial publication.
steps: Whisk gram flour with enough water to make a smooth batter that falls through a medium-hole boondi ladle in round drops. Rest for 10 minutes.
Prepare a one-string syrup with sugar and 3/4 cup water, about 108–110°C, and flavour it with cardamom. Keep it hot but not boiling.
Heat oil or ghee to 170–175°C. Hold the perforated ladle above the oil and pour a small amount of batter onto it, tapping gently to release round droplets.
